summary: Franklin County Commission denied rezoning for Red Dog Technologies' proposed Bitcoin mining data center near Tullahoma. Residents said noise would be unbearable and damage property values. The denial was one of the early examples of local opposition to crypto mining data centers in Tennessee.

Roundhouse Digital Infrastructure planned a $100M, 10MW data center at the Port of Cascade Locks in the Columbia River Gorge, about 40 miles east of Portland. The facility would have more than doubled the area's power draw. Residents raised concerns about electricity supply impacts, the developer's lack of experience, and unclear funding. Opposition was a key issue in local elections that saw voters recall two officials who backed the project, with two other data center advocates losing re-election bids. Following the recall, the new port authority board canceled the project in July 2023. Friends of the Columbia Gorge was a key opposition group.

opposition_groups: Coalition to Protect Prince William County
summary: The PW Digital Gateway proposes rezoning 2,100+ acres near Manassas Battlefield for up to 18 million sq ft of data centers (10.5M by Compass, 7.9M by QTS), supporting 1,471 to 5,048 jobs. A judge voided the $24.7B rezoning and the Virginia Court of Appeals barred construction pending appeal. The Coalition to Protect Prince William County and American Battlefield Trust lead opposition. The project strongly encourages closed-loop cooling systems to minimize water impact.

Amazon Web Services is building a $500M data center on 243 acres along Route 3 in Stevensburg, Culpeper County, with two buildings totaling 427,000 sq ft and approximately 90MW capacity. The project anticipates 90 permanent jobs (45 per building) and 365 construction workers. Board of Supervisors approved the rezoning 4-3 despite Planning Commission voting 5-4 against. Coalition to Save Culpeper, Save Brandy Station, and Piedmont Environmental Council filed a lawsuit alleging illegal spot-zoning.

Tract withdrew its $14B, 1,000-acre 'Project Range' datacenter proposal in western Maricopa County in May 2024 after the adjacent cities of Goodyear and Buckeye formally opposed the rezoning, citing incompatibility with surrounding residential neighborhoods. Building heights and noise were key concerns raised by city staff.

Both Goodyear and Buckeye city governments formally opposed Tract's $14B datacenter rezoning in the adjacent unincorporated Maricopa County area. City staff outlined concerns about building heights, noise, and emergency resources, leading to the project's withdrawal. Tract later proposed a new 1,700-acre complex in Buckeye near the airport with mayoral support.

Provident Realty Advisors proposed a $1.3B data center on the 200-acre former Brassie Golf Course site along Pearson Road. The plan called for eight buildings at 150,000 sq ft each with backup generators. At a Memorial Day meeting, 60-75 residents gathered at Dogwood Park voicing concerns about noise, air quality, water, wetlands, wildlife, property values, and mental health impacts. The developer proposed landscaping buffers and noise suppressors. The Town Council declared it could 'never support this project' at its current scale, and Provident abandoned the proposal on June 10, 2024. The developer subsequently moved to Burns Harbor with a similar proposal.

DeKalb County approved a 100-day moratorium on data center applications and permits in July 2024, later extended multiple times — to October 2025, then December 2025, and finally to June 2026. Commissioner Edward 'Ted' Terry warned that residential power bills could rise by an average of $20/month if data centers expand unchecked. Residents raised concerns about noise, pollution, environmental impacts, and equity, describing data centers as 'unsustainable and harmful to health.' The county currently has no restrictions, meaning data centers could be built near Doraville, Lithonia, and school campuses. The county is developing proposed regulations to address data center siting and impacts.

opposition_groups: Clean Air Coalition of Western New York
summary: North Tonawanda Common Council unanimously approved a two-year moratorium on all crypto mining and data center developments in July 2024. The ban covers new crypto mining plants, expansion of existing ones, and broadly includes AI processing, general data computing, and other large-scale electronic facilities. The goal is to update the city's zoning codes to address high noise levels from existing crypto mining operations. A separate court victory rescinded approval of a fracked gas power plant sale to a cryptocurrency mining company (Digihost) in the city. Digihost agreed to reimburse up to $30,000 for the city's zoning update costs.

Provident Realty Advisors — after being rejected in Chesterton — proposed a seven-building, 102-acre data center campus at the corner of S. Babcock and Haglund Roads on the Worthington Steel property. Buildings could reach 120 feet tall. Over 100 people attended the August 5 Planning Commission meeting, with 20 speaking against the proposal citing unclear community benefits, fire risks, noise, and impact on the nearby Indiana Dunes National Park. The Plan Commission postponed its vote amid public outcry. As of February 2025, Provident announced intentions to revise its proposal and conduct further community outreach, with no formal application advanced.

On September 3, 2024, the Atlanta City Council voted to ban data centers within a half-mile of MARTA rail stops and along the 22-mile Beltline trail loop. Councilmembers Jason Dozier and Matt Westmoreland sponsored the legislation to preserve mixed-use development, housing, trails, and green space in prime neighborhoods like Downtown and Grove Park. Mayor Andre Dickens supported the ban, stating data centers limit jobs, strain utilities, and negatively impact communities. Existing data centers operated by QTS and DataBank in the Beltline Overlay district are grandfathered. The council later rejected a separate data center proposal in December 2024.

Fairfax County Board of Supervisors voted 8-2 on September 10, 2024 to adopt a comprehensive Data Center Zoning Ordinance Amendment. Key provisions: data centers must be built at least 200 feet from residential areas (500 feet for ground-mounted equipment like generators); minimum 1-mile distance from Metro stations; all cooling/ventilating equipment must be fully enclosed; mandatory noise studies before site plan approval; new architectural requirements for building design; 80,000 sq ft size limits in some districts; Special Exception permits required for most new data centers. Site plans accepted before July 16, 2024 are grandfathered under prior rules.

Thorndale passed a formal resolution opposing a proposed 15MW bitcoin mining facility by Data Factory Austin on County Road 437 in Milam County. Dozens of residents expressed concerns about noise, water supply, and energy consumption (15MW equals about 10,500 homes' daily electricity). Residents referenced noise complaints from Data Factory's existing 10MW mine near Rockdale, where noise peaked at 99 decibels — louder than a subway. The city signed the resolution to show solidarity with citizens and sent it to local and state leaders to prevent construction.

Peculiar originally added a 'data center' definition to its light industrial zoning, enabling Diode Ventures' proposed $1.5B Harper Road Technology Park — a 500-acre campus with multiple buildings next to homes and businesses. A Facebook group called 'Don't Dump Data in Peculiar' grew to nearly 1,000 members (about 1/6 of the city's population). After hundreds of residents called on local leaders to reverse course, the Planning Commission Board announced on October 3, 2024 an ordinance removing data centers from the zoning code, effectively blocking the project. The mayor claimed aldermen were pressured by a 'boisterous and ill-informed group.'

opposition_groups: Gilmer County Citizens Against Crypto Mining
summary: Gilmer County in the North Georgia mountains became the only Georgia county to enact a full ban on cryptocurrency mining. The ban followed organized opposition led by Cyndie Roberson, cofounder of 'Gilmer County Citizens Against Crypto Mining,' who brought hundreds of residents to a planning commission meeting. A Change.org petition ('Stop Gilmer County from allowing a Crypto Mining facility in our county') gathered signatures. Opponents cited extreme noise, massive electricity and water drain, and insufficient job creation. The county unanimously passed crypto and land use ordinances on first reading.

SoftBank subsidiary SB Energy is building and operating OpenAI's 1.2GW Stargate datacenter in Milam County. While no organized local opposition group has been identified, broader Texas concerns about datacenter water and energy consumption apply. SB Energy plans to build new generation capacity to support the facility and claims it will minimize water usage and protect Texas ratepayers.

The No Desert Data Center Coalition organized fierce opposition to Beale Infrastructure's 750MW Project Blue datacenter in Marana. Despite collecting over 2,800 petition signatures in just four days and filing a lawsuit alleging Open Meeting Act violations, the Marana Town Council unanimously approved the rezoning in January 2026. The coalition has expanded to oppose multiple Southern Arizona datacenter projects and has traveled nationally to share their organizing playbook.

Over 200 people packed the Betty Hagel Memorial Civic Center in Center, ND as Oliver County leaders debated a proposed moratorium on data centers and cryptocurrency mining. Residents voiced concerns about noise, heat, space, and rising utility rates, demanding to know who is building data centers and their long-term plans. The zoning board voted 4-3 to recommend a 3-year moratorium, which was formally adopted by the County Commission on December 12, 2024 covering both solar projects and data center facilities. Minnkota Power submitted a letter opposing the moratorium.

Amazon's $10 billion data center campus in Canton, the largest capital investment in Mississippi history, occupies 1,713 acres across two sites in Madison County, with 927 acres at the Madison County Mega Site off Highway 22 and Nissan Parkway. The campus is expected to create 1,000 high-wage jobs. In partnership with Entergy, AWS will bring 650 megawatts of new renewable energy. Idlebriar subdivision residents, less than 200 yards from the campus, report dust, traffic, noise, and inability to sell homes. Amazon offered free fiber internet and car washes as mitigation.

Sierra Club Toiyabe Chapter appealed the Planning Commission's approval of Webb Data Center's 82,000 sq ft facility, arguing the city established zoning guidelines without public input. The City Council denied the appeal. Commissioner Becerra advocated for a comprehensive data center ordinance. Former City Manager Doug Thornley registered as a lobbyist for the data center project, raising conflict of interest concerns.

opposition_groups: Coalition for Responsible Data Center Development
summary: Tract received approval for a 708MW hyperscale data center consisting of 12 buildings up to 80 feet tall and 2.53 million sq ft, plus an electrical substation and several hundred generators with over a million gallons of stored fuel. The project would double the city's water consumption at up to 2.93 million gallons/day. Officials signed NDAs with Tract. Castle Rock Township sued for breach of the Orderly Annexation Agreement on Jan. 14, 2025. The Coalition for Responsible Data Center Development, a 501(c)(3)/(c)(4) non-profit founded by Kathy Johnson, organized opposition. Environmental Coalition of Farmington MN members packed council chambers. State legislators introduced a bill to regulate hyperscale data center zoning.

OpenAI's Stargate project — announced by President Trump as a $500B AI infrastructure initiative — is building its first campus on 875 acres in Abilene. The facility will draw up to 1.2 GW of power across eight 500,000 sq ft 'AI factory' halls. A $500M on-site Lancium natural gas power plant will pollute the surrounding community, putting residents at increased risk of asthma and respiratory disease. The project has sparked a housing crisis — thousands of construction workers flooded the city, driving up rents and forcing vulnerable people onto the streets. Despite $100B+ in investment, only 357 permanent jobs are promised (avg salary $57,600). Crusoe received an 85% property tax abatement on billions of infrastructure, causing Abilene to forgo enormous potential revenue. Former school board member Samuel Garcia was among the few vocal opponents questioning water and power impacts.

company: Stream Data Centers (via Eagle Myra LLC)
summary: During a rare winter storm on January 22, 2025, Marion County Council approved a $2.4B data center under the code name 'Project Liberty' while most residents were snowed in and unaware. The project is backed by Stream Data Centers, operating through a legal entity called Eagle Myra, LLC. Key personnel include Anthony Bolner (founding partner) and Osin O Murchu (chief development officer). Council members had signed NDAs barring public disclosure. At the meeting, community members pressed council members on how little the public knew. Joel Rogers, a council member, spoke against the project. The case became a national symbol of how developers use NDAs and secrecy to push projects through communities — often majority-Black rural counties — with less organized political power. Marion County is predominantly Black.

Marshall County became the first Indiana county to pass a data center moratorium in early February 2025. Commissioner and zoning board member Stan Klotz pushed for the pause, citing concerns about developments in nearby areas like New Carlisle. Residents voiced concerns about water use, environmental impact, and effects on electricity bills. The moratorium was followed by White County (October) and Putnam County (November).

DC BLOX built a 120MW hyperscale facility (Atlanta West) in Lithia Springs in Douglas County, adjacent to Microsoft and Google facilities. DC BLOX reportedly experienced community opposition that derailed a different project in the area, and held at least two dozen meetings with county officials and community groups. Amazon also purchased 118 acres in Douglas County for a possible datacenter amid sustainability concerns.

In March 2025, the Loudoun County Board of Supervisors voted 7-2 to approve a comprehensive plan amendment and zoning ordinance amendment eliminating by-right data center development. All new data center applications now require staff-level review, a public hearing, and Planning Commission recommendation. Previously, data centers could be approved with only staff-level review in some zones. Loudoun Climate Project and the Virginia Data Center Reform Coalition (which includes the Piedmont Environmental Council, Sierra Club, and National Parks Conservation Association) pushed for these restrictions.

Fundamental Data, a Virginia-based company, applied for an air permit for the 'Ridgeline Facility' — a natural gas power plant and data center campus between Thomas and Davis that could span 10,000 acres across Tucker and Grant counties. The permit application was heavily redacted, preventing residents from quantifying environmental impacts. Tucker United formed in March 2025 and mobilized hundreds of opponents. Over 2,000 people signed a petition to Governor Morrissey asking him to veto HB 2014, a law stripping local control over 'high-impact' data centers. Tucker United, the West Virginia Highlands Conservancy, and the Sierra Club appealed the DEP air permit, but the Air Quality Board sided with the developers in February 2026. Protesters traveled to Charleston to demand lawmakers restore local authority. HB 2014 diverts property tax revenue away from local bodies.

A Meta data center spanning 715,000 sq ft under construction in Middleton Township was revealed in April 2025, anticipated operational in 2027 with 100 employees. Nearby communities preemptively enacted moratoriums in response to data center activity in northwest Ohio.

Project Sail is a 900MW, $17B proposed hyperscale data center on 832 acres in northwest Coweta County by Prologis/Atlas Development. Citizens for Rural Coweta formed in early January 2025 at Sargent Baptist Church and grew to 3,800+ members. The Stop Project Sail campaign gathered 2,245 petition signatures. Rae Lynn Butler, secretary of culture and humanities for the Muscogee Nation, opposed the project due to historic Muscogee Nation land. The project would consume 9 million gallons of water per day. Coweta County passed a moratorium on data center development.

Conservation groups sued BLM to halt the Greenlink West transmission line being built largely to serve massive data center demand (4,000+ MW committed) in the Reno area, alleging federal environmental law violations.

Amazon planned a multibillion-dollar data center near the Xcel Sherco coal-fired power plant in Becker, proposing 250 diesel backup generators. In March 2025, the Minnesota PUC ruled Amazon must obtain a Certificate of Need, placing the burden on Amazon to demonstrate necessity and evaluate sustainable alternatives. Environmental advocate Sarah Mooradian of CURE MN argued that even generators not connected to the grid have huge impacts. In May 2025, Amazon abandoned the project, citing uncertain development timelines for permitting. Minnesota also eliminated a sales tax exemption on data center electricity.

On June 10, 2025, the Henrico County Board of Supervisors adopted stricter data center requirements, creating a 3,100-acre White Oak Technology Park overlay as the only zone where data centers can be built without a Provisional Use Permit (PUP). Outside this zone, all data center proposals require PUPs with public hearings. New requirements include restrictions on backup generator testing hours, mandatory enclosure of noise-generating equipment, and closed-loop water-cooling systems. The Board also denied a zoning appeal from Centra Logistics for a data center outside the overlay.
Surge Development withdrew its application to rezone 775 acres of farmland for a data center near Tuttle Orchards, a century-old farm. Over 70 people signed up to speak at a public hearing.
City approved five data centers plus Google and QTS sites. Meta withdrew 35M+ gallons of water in 2024 under confidentiality. Residents pushed back against nuclear zoning plan.

Little Rock approved annexation and rezoning under the code name 'Project Boar.' AVAIO Digital Partners (Stamford, CT) later announced a 760-acre campus with $6B first phase investment and over $21B through full development. The campus will create 500+ permanent operations jobs over five years. Entergy has contracted 150MW to be energized in Q2 2027. Arkansas Grass Roots United and planning commissioners objected to the secrecy, lack of public input, and potential environmental impacts.

Cielo Digital Infrastructure is investing $2.1 billion in an 86-acre campus off Ford Road in Gaffney, engineered for up to 300MW of IT load. Four facilities of approximately 400,000 sq ft each (1.6M total) with a dedicated on-site electrical substation. The project will create 30 new jobs. Initial operations expected online by year-end 2028. A regional petition opposing Upstate SC data centers has gathered approximately 4,000 signatures.

company: University of Michigan / Los Alamos National Laboratory / DTE Energy
project_name: Hydro Park Data Center / AI Supercomputer
summary: A $1.2 billion data center project on South Hydro Park by the University of Michigan, Los Alamos National Laboratory, and DTE Energy would include a 100MW facility for classified national security AI work and a 10MW UMich research facility on 144.5 acres of critical wetland near Ford Lake Dam. The opposition group 'Stop the Data Center' organized assemblies and protests. Ypsilanti Township pushed back, urging relocation.

Oakland University's Board of Trustees approved plans for a data center on Parking Lot 35, located behind Hillcrest Residence Hall near the Native American Heritage Site, biological preserve, and nature trail. Student Congress led opposition efforts through an Action Network petition. Students and community members cite concerns about water usage, chemical pollution, and impacts on nearby dorm residents.

Millard County Commission unanimously rezoned 1,100+ acres from agricultural to heavy industrial for Creekstone Energy's 10GW data center campus despite significant public opposition. The county is under 100% drought conditions per NOAA. Residents argue the rezone permanently shifts the area's rural character and threatens wildlife. Large data centers in the area could use up to 5 million gallons of water per day. Creekstone and Joule Power claim closed-loop cooling and water recycling systems will achieve 90% water reduction, but residents remain skeptical.

status: enacted - POWER Act (HB 3546) passed with bipartisan support (41-16) and signed by Gov. Kotek in 2025; HB 4084 (2026) data center tax break provision paused
summary: Oregon's POWER Act (HB 3546) passed in 2025 with strong bipartisan support, creating a new classification for data centers and large industrial energy users (20+ MW) requiring them to pay their fair share of electricity costs. Requires 10-year utility purchase agreements. In 2026, Gov. Kotek's HB 4084 Enterprise Zone tax break expansion initially included data centers but that provision was cut amid concerns, with Kotek's office supporting the pause to 'address immediate concerns.'

Warrenton Town Council voted to fully ban data centers after significant community opposition. GI Partners had purchased a $39M, 42-acre plot for a 220,000 sq ft data center. Over 500 people attended a Town Council meeting with nearly 130 speakers opposing the project, including Oscar winner Robert Duvall. Citizens for Fauquier County (CFFC) filed multiple lawsuits, including challenges over the special use permit and withheld emails.

Google confirmed it is behind a data center project on 550 acres along West State Road 42 near Monrovia. Despite resident protests, the Morgan County Plan Commission gave a favorable recommendation for rezoning. County commissioners approved additional land. Protect Morgan County group is raising funds for legal action, and residents filed a lawsuit claiming commissioners disregarded the county's comprehensive plan and that the rezoning was arbitrary. Construction began in February 2026. The Citizens Action Coalition (CAC) is also actively fighting the project.

Western Hospitality Partners proposed 'Project Lincoln: OC Data Center,' a $6B, 600MW facility initially planned for 267 acres at 3557 North Highway 53. Due to fierce opposition from 'We Are Oldham County,' the company tried switching to a smaller 30-acre drive-in movie theater site. Oldham County passed a 150-day moratorium 4-2, and Western Hospitality Partners subsequently withdrew its application entirely.

project_name: AWS Data Center at 8050 Camino Arroyo
summary: Amazon Web Services received approval on July 3, 2025 for a 218,000 sq ft data center requiring 49 MW from PG&E and 25 emergency generators on a 56-acre site. Two Change.org petitions were created - one to stop the project and another to ban/regulate data centers in Gilroy. The mayor said the city cannot legally stop the project.

opposition_groups: Arizona Chapter of 7x24 Exchange (industry opposition to restrictions)
summary: Mesa City Council adopted new zoning rules restricting data centers to General and High Industrial zones (4.1% of city land), requiring special use permits. Fifteen data centers had been constructed, approved, or proposed in Mesa within six years.
St. Charles enacted a full moratorium on data center development.

Thor Equities proposed a $1 billion data center on 810 acres (building on about 20%) near Milan. Despite the Planning Commission recommending denial, the Board of Trustees unanimously approved rezoning of 522 acres in July 2025. PACT (Protect Augusta Charter Township) gathered 957 signatures in two weeks (needed 561) to force a public referendum. The special election is expected May 2026 at earliest. PACT is confident residents will vote the project down. Opponents fear noise, light pollution, destruction of rural character, higher utility bills, and brownouts. Thor committed to abiding by the township's noise ordinance.

Oppidan halted its data center project citing lengthy permitting for diesel generators. MCEA (Minnesota Center for Environmental Advocacy) sued the city in August 2025, alleging officials hid information and approved a faulty AUAR (Alternative Urban Areawide Review) environmental study. MCEA argues the review failed to adequately analyze water withdrawals from the Mount Simon-Hinckley Aquifer that supplies drinking water to ~1 million Minnesotans. The proposed development could consume 500,000 to 30 million gallons of water/day. Even though Oppidan backed away, the lawsuit remains necessary because the approved AUAR could allow future data center proposals. Briefing expected April 2026.

Maryland-based developer Natelli Investments LLC filed plans for the 'New Hill Digital Campus,' a 300MW data center complex on 190 acres off Old U.S. Highway 1 near Apex, requiring annexation and rezoning. The facility would consume up to 1 million gallons of reclaimed water per day during peak summer heat. Over 4,500 people signed a Change.org petition opposing the project. Residents formed the Protect Wake County Coalition to coordinate opposition. Concerns include noise, air pollution from backup generators, water use, strain on the power grid, loss of farmland, and impact on nearby schools. The neighboring Chatham County approved a 12-month moratorium on data centers and crypto mining in response to the regional proposal wave.

opposition_groups: NAACP Center for Environmental and Climate Justice
summary: Logistics Land Investment proposed Project Marvel, a $14.5B, 4.5 million sq ft data center requiring clearing 700 acres of forest in Bessemer — a city that is approximately 70% Black with a legacy of pollution from steel mills, coal plants, and coke ovens. The NAACP (Alabama State Conference, Metro Birmingham Branch, and Center for Environmental and Climate Justice) formally opposed the project, demanding transparency, disclosure of NDAs, expected employment and tax impacts, energy/water usage details, and guarantees against utility cost increases. The project would require 2 million gallons of water daily. Despite stiff opposition and 7,000+ petition signatures, Bessemer changed its laws to accommodate data centers in October 2025, passing the project forward.
Developer withdrew $1B rezoning application after 8 months of resident organizing. Planning Commission had backed project in May. Opposition cited industrialization of rural land.

Applied Digital's $7B Polaris Forge 1 campus in Ellendale hosts a 250MW data center leased to CoreWeave for 15 years. Construction is underway despite community resistance. At a packed city meeting, residents wearing 'No Eminent Domain' t-shirts and carrying 'My Land, My Choice' signs confronted elected officials about lack of representation. Renee Hutchinson, who retired to Ellendale before the facility was announced, cited grid stability concerns. Leon Westerhausen opposed the project, warning it would affect future land development. The facility requested annexation into the city of Ellendale in late 2024. North Dakota lawmakers are now considering legislation to address rifts between infrastructure developers and landowners.

Elon Musk's xAI built 'Colossus,' the world's largest AI supercomputer, in Boxtown — a 90% Black neighborhood in southwest Memphis first settled by formerly enslaved people in 1863. The facility operates 33 methane-powered gas turbines (permitted for only 15) without appropriate Clean Air Act permits, increasing Memphis's smog-forming NOx emissions by 30-60%. University of Tennessee researchers found peak nitrogen dioxide levels increased 79% near the facility. Over 2,000 public comments were submitted to the Shelby County Health Department opposing the turbines. The SELC filed a 60-day notice of intent to sue on behalf of the NAACP for Clean Air Act violations. Despite protests, the Shelby County Health Department approved xAI's air quality permit. Community groups — including Memphis Community Against Pollution, Protect Our Aquifer, and Young Gifted & Green — formed a coalition opposing the project. State Rep. Justin J. Pearson has been a vocal opponent. xAI leased 552 acres in Frank C. Pidgeon Industrial Park and plans to expand to 300MW+ consuming 5.7 million gallons of water per day. The facility's MLGW electricity bills averaged $4.5 million/month in 2025.

Microsoft filed plans for two data centers ($350M each) near Castroville while Rowan Digital Infrastructure's Project Cinco is developing a separate $900M hyperscale campus on 440 acres with 300MW capacity. Medina County is in an 'exceptional drought' and the projects would draw water from the Edwards Aquifer. Rowan announced investment in an Edwards Aquifer Authority conservation program to achieve 250% offset of water usage. Eight data centers total are planned for the county, making it a major hotspot. Concerns include traffic, water usage, and environmental impacts near elementary schools.

Denver-based Tract proposed the $30B Mooresville Technology Park on 400 acres of undeveloped land owned by Teresa Earnhardt, widow of NASCAR champion Dale Earnhardt. The project drew intense community backlash. Dale Earnhardt's eldest son Kerry and his wife Rene posted a YouTube video detailing their opposition, saying their father would be 'furious' to see his name associated with the project. About 200 people crowded a Board of Commissioners meeting wearing red T-shirts to signal opposition, with all 10 speakers raising concerns about water use in a drought-prone region and skepticism about job promises. Tract withdrew the rezoning request on August 13, 2025.

Recall effort against Mayor Ted Neitzke failed, falling several hundred signatures short of the 25% threshold by the Feb. 16 deadline. However, Great Lakes Neighbors United's separate lawsuit challenging $458M in TIF financing (TID) continues, with a motion hearing scheduled for March 13. The group also submitted a referendum petition requiring voter approval for future TIDs over $10M. Business associations counter-sued over the referendum ordinance. The $15B data center campus (Vantage Data Centers/OpenAI/Oracle/Stargate) construction is underway.

Copia Power Devco LLC (backed by Carlyle) received 4-1 approval from Lyon County commissioners for a Master Plan Amendment to build the Monarch Data Center - 4.6 million sq ft across 14 buildings on 505 acres in Mason Valley, Yerington. The 1,000MW facility would require enough electricity to power roughly 500,000 homes. Multiple Change.org petitions oppose the project, citing water scarcity in the drought-prone region, corruption allegations against officials, and impacts on the agricultural community. Commissioner Hockaday cast the lone dissenting vote.
Tucson City Council voted 7-0 to reject Project Blue, a 290-acre, 600MW data center linked to AWS. Community opposition centered on water use and energy costs. Amazon later pulled out entirely.

Wrangell's borough assembly authorized a nonbinding agreement with Greensparc to explore a small-scale data center using hydroelectric power. Community members raised concerns about noise, light, and water discharge. A Cordova resident reported Greensparc's existing facility there has been 'underwhelming and underperforming.'

NorthPoint Development (Kansas City) proposed a 664-acre, 14-building hyperscale data center with 1,008MW peak power demand and 3 million sq ft in the small riverside community of fewer than 2,000 people. The projected electricity consumption of 8.8 million megawatt-hours per year is more than half the output of the Gaston Plant (1,880MW). Over 100 residents packed a council meeting to oppose. A Change.org petition gathered 500+ signatures. No formal application has been filed but residents are organizing preemptively.

Applied Digital's $3B Polaris Forge 2 data center (280MW) proposed near the 900-person town of Harwood on approximately 900 acres sparked a confrontational community meeting on August 25, 2025. Residents peppered the company VP with questions for 90 minutes, raising concerns about noise, utility rate increases, sprawl, and lack of transparency (NDAs required for council members). Harwood approved the project in September and construction began shortly after. The project triggered an annexation battle with the City of Fargo, which sought to annex the site for tax revenue, though Fargo later withdrew its annexation attempts.

opposition_groups: Empowerment Congress of Dona Ana County
summary: County Commission advanced 4-1 preliminary approval for BorderPlex Digital Assets' $165B Project Jupiter data center near Santa Teresa on approximately 1,400 acres. The project was initially a $50B proposal that grew to $165B total. Each data center building is projected to use approximately 625,000 gallons of water per day via closed-loop cooling. The Empowerment Congress of Dona Ana County filed a lawsuit challenging the approval process, questioning water impacts, oversight, and approval speed.

company: TA Realty / Digital Realty (recently approved projects)
summary: Clayton County Board of Commissioners approved a moratorium on new data centers.

opposition_groups: Troup County Anti-Data Center Coalition (Gage Bailey, founder)
summary: Troup County enacted a moratorium on data center development.

opposition_groups: No Data Center in Landover coalition
Party for Socialism and Liberation DC
summary: Prince George's County paused data center approvals amid policy review.
LaGrange City Council passed a 180-day moratorium on data centers.
Pike County enacted a moratorium on data center development as part of a wave of Georgia county ordinances.
Lamar County enacted a moratorium on data center development.

Compass Data Centers proposed a $1B+ campus on 333 acres of farmland on Stamey Farm Road in western Iredell County. Plans include five buildings of approximately 270,000 sq ft each (1.35M total) with 40 backup generators per building. A Facebook group with 900+ members organized opposition citing air and groundwater pollution and property value impacts. Despite opposition, the Statesville City Council unanimously approved the project in September 2025.

Hampden Township Commissioners unanimously rejected a proposed zoning ordinance amendment that would have designated office-park zones for data centers. Some 300 residents signed a petition, and commissioners heard more than two hours of public comment from dozens of people, most opposing the change. Food & Water Watch's eastern Pennsylvania organizer Virginia Marcille-Kerslake mobilized opposition through a door-to-door campaign with flyers and QR codes. Residents raised concerns about air, light, and noise impacts. The rejection was part of a broader wave of Pennsylvania communities proactively restricting data centers.

status: advancing - A5462 passed both Assembly and Senate (Jan 12, 2026), headed to Governor's desk
opposition_groups: New Jersey League of Conservation Voters
summary: New Jersey has introduced multiple data center bills. S4318 (Sen. Burzichelli, Sen. Greenstein) requires the BPU to study data center effects on electricity costs and was signed into law by Gov. Murphy. S4293 (Sen. Ruiz, Sen. Burgess) requires data centers to report water and energy usage to the BPU; conditionally vetoed by Gov. Murphy in October 2025. A5462/S4307 (Asm. Dave Bailey Jr.) would require electric utilities to develop special tariff rules for data centers using 100+ MW monthly to protect non-data-center customers from cost increases; pocket-vetoed by Gov. Murphy as one of his last acts and refiled in the 2026 session. In February 2026, committees in both chambers approved a bill requiring large power users (100+ MW/month) to pay at least 85% of their service costs.

opposition_groups: Eco-Socialist Green Party of Eastern Missouri
Democratic Socialists of America St. Louis
Party for Socialism and Liberation St. Louis
summary: A $600M data center proposed at the Armory in St. Louis spurred a petition with 12,300+ signatures calling for a year-long moratorium. Over 200 residents attended a community-led town hall. The Board of Aldermen passed Resolution 111 (9-2) to regulate data centers, but a proposed outright ban was voted down. Opposition argues the resolution still allows projects to proceed.

A data center proposed at a former power station site in Carbon County's Packer and Banks townships has been delayed by officials. Opposition groups Save Carbon County and Greenfire Coalition have spoken out, with two petitions gathering around 900 signatures total. Concerns focus on loss of green spaces and community impacts.
Trustees unanimously approved a 9-month moratorium after residents complained about noise from existing Amazon facilities and concerns about water and electricity costs.
Neighbors questioned why a 12MW data center was located in their neighborhood, raising concerns about pollution and community impact after RadiusDC broke ground.

company: Poe Companies / PowerHouse Data Centers (approved project)
summary: One 525MW data center project in southwest Louisville has been cleared to proceed, built by Poe Companies and PowerHouse Data Centers (starting at 100MW, expanding to 400MW). District 17 Council Member Markus Winkler proposed a six-month moratorium on September 11, 2025, which was tabled indefinitely. A survey showed 81.5% of respondents chose 'Nowhere in Jefferson County' for data center locations. City planners continue drafting regulations.

Developers proposed a data center campus offering up to 1.5GW of capacity on 209 acres in Blakely Borough — requiring power equivalent to the entire output of the nearby Lackawanna Energy Center gas-fired power plant. The first phase called for four two-story buildings at 150,000 sq ft per floor. About 100 residents packed the Borough Building with 60 more outside and 210+ watching via Zoom for an August 13, 2025 Q&A session. A grassroots petition gathered roughly 800 signatures opposing the project. The developers withdrew their zoning application on September 12, 2025, days before a scheduled public hearing. The borough subsequently adopted an ordinance applying nearly three dozen restrictions on future data center developments.

College Station residents mounted strong opposition to Priority Power's proposed 600MW datacenter on 200 acres of Midtown Drive. More than 75 individuals registered to speak in person and a petition with over 5,000 signatures was submitted. Concerns included noise pollution, procedural issues, environmental impacts, and utility demands. The city rejected the land sale and is now proactively considering guardrails for future datacenter proposals.

Google withdrew its 468-acre 'Project Flo' data center hours before the council was expected to vote it down. 15 of 25 council members announced opposition. A 7,000+ signature petition and loud cheers erupted at the withdrawal.

Google withdrew its $1B 'Project Flo' data center proposal minutes before the City-County Council vote on September 22, 2025, after months of community pushback. Hundreds attended to protest the 468-acre farmland conversion. Residents cited water/electricity consumption, environmental risks, and limited permanent jobs.

Menomonie signed an NDA with Balloonist LLC in February 2024, over a year before announcing the $1.6B data center proposal in July 2025. Over the following six months, residents coordinated a local opposition campaign. Mayor Randy Knaack announced on Sept. 22 that the city was no longer interested in pursuing the project. On January 5, 2026, the City Council revised a zoning ordinance to bar Balloonist from moving ahead. The data center would have used approximately 75,000 gallons of water per day and brought 50-75 full-time jobs.

TeraWulf signed an 80-year lease for 183 acres at a former coal plant on Cayuga Lake to develop up to 400 MW of data center capacity. Hundreds of residents submitted public comments opposing the project, and the town held a public hearing on a moratorium to pause large-scale development while rewriting the zoning code.

opposition_groups: Taylor community residents (led by Carrie D'Anna)
summary: Five residents sued over a data center on land deeded for parkland. Court temporarily blocked it but dismissed the lawsuit, ruling residents lacked standing.

Fidelis New Energy proposed a data center complex in Mason County, but residents are frustrated by the lack of answers about impacts on water, environment, and public health. State law HB 2014 stripped local control over 'high-impact' data centers and diverts property tax revenue away from local bodies. Residents from Mason, Tucker, Mingo, and Logan counties visited the Capitol in Feb 2026 to demand lawmakers restore local control.

White County's Board of Commissioners passed a moratorium on data center development on October 20, 2025, after residents packed public meetings in opposition. The moratorium was prompted by a proposed project near Wolcott. Residents voiced concerns about water availability and process transparency. White County joined Marshall (February) and Putnam (November) as Indiana's three counties with data center moratoriums, which conflict with a state government actively courting tech projects through a 2019 law granting sizable tax credits for computer equipment purchases.

The Yorkville City Council unanimously approved Project Cardinal on Nov. 10, 2025 -- a 1,037-acre data center campus with 14 two-story warehouses, developed by Pioneer Development LLC. However, a resident filed a lawsuit arguing the project would cause sound pollution and decreased property values. An online petition opposing both Project Cardinal and a separate Project Steel in Yorkville gathered over 1,300 signatures. The lawsuit appeared headed for settlement after a Dec. 30 hearing. NOTE: This appears to be Yorkville, IL (Kendall County), not Yorkville, WI.

opposition_groups: No Data Centers in Jones County
summary: EagleRock Partners withdrew rezoning for 600+ acres after organized opposition under 'No Data Centers in Jones County.' Commissioners voted 4-1 for a 90-day moratorium.

opposition_groups: Concerned Citizens of Twiggs County (Rob Fricks, attorney)
summary: Twiggs County commissioners unanimously approved Eagle Rock Partners' $5B datacenter rezoning despite hundreds of residents packing the meeting in opposition. The crowd erupted in boos after the vote. Landowners filed a lawsuit alleging improper zoning notices (wrong property, incorrect hearing date) and failure to conduct a Development of Regional Impact study. Construction cannot begin until 2028 due to power infrastructure delays.
Planning & Zoning Commission considering industrial rezoning for potential 200 MW data center. Environmental group opposing, citing lack of transparency on impacts.

The Page City Council voted 5-2 to sell 500 acres less than one mile from Horseshoe Bend to UK-based Huntley LLC for a $10B, 1GW data center. Residents filed a referendum with 409 hand-signed signatures, but the city rejected it on a technicality. Beth Henshaw gathered over 400 additional signatures to put the issue on a future ballot. Online petitions gathered over 1,800 signatures from a town of only 7,000 people. Lake Powell is approximately 26% full, and residents are deeply concerned about water draw from the Colorado River, which is already stressed and over-allocated.

Over 30 Carroll County residents filed a lawsuit in Superior Court challenging Villa Rica's annexation and rezoning of 200+ acres for a data center. Door-to-door petitions gathered 500 signatures with approximately 95% of respondents opposing the project. Concerns include 1.1 million gallons/day water use and proximity to residential areas.

Over 100 residents from New York and New Jersey objected to DataBank's proposal for a 146,000 sq-ft data center adjacent to Lake Tappan reservoir. The Bi-State Datacenter Crisis Coalition organized opposition citing threats to wetlands, wildlife, water quality, and fire risks from lithium-ion battery storage.

Preble County Planning Commission unanimously (11-0) denied rezoning of 300 acres of farmland for a data center. The developer withdrew his application in October after residents organized a referendum campaign. Former Republican state lawmaker Gene Krebs helped lead opposition.

Prologis purchased 141 acres for its 'Project Mila' datacenter in Trenton. Residents Barry and Lorie Blankenship formed W.A.T.E.R. (Woodsdale and Trenton Environmental Resistance), which had 248 Facebook members. The group filed multiple public records requests seeking information about NDAs, environmental analyses, and health studies. Residents expressed concerns about higher energy/water bills, noise and light pollution, and feeling left in the dark.

A Fortune 20 tech company (identity hidden behind NDAs) proposed a $1 billion, 1,200-megawatt data center on up to 5,000 acres of farmland on Big Pond Pike near Maysville. The project could bring 400 full-time positions plus 1,500 construction jobs with $80,000 average salary. The massive power requirement nearly equals the output of Eastern Kentucky Power Cooperative's Spurlock power plant. Town leaders signed NDAs barring public disclosure, sparking controversy. Rules governing the project moved toward a final vote in February 2026.

Project Kestrel proposes six hyperscale datacenters totaling 1.8 million sq ft north of Kansas City International Airport with massive property and sales tax subsidies. Platte County Presiding Commissioner Scott Fricker publicly opposes the project, stating it will drive up water and utility rates for households and small businesses while giving subsidies to the world's richest corporations. Residents lack clarity on who is behind the project, who pays for power upgrades, and why there was no public input.

project_name: Lower Mt. Bethel Data Center Campus
summary: Peron Development and J.G. Petrucci Company are proposing a data center campus at the intersection of Gravel Hill Road and Martins Creek Belvidere Highway in Lower Mt. Bethel Township, Northampton County. The site is across from the John M. Mauser Nature Education Trail System along the scenic Delaware River. A petition gathered ~4,600 signatures opposing the project.

opposition_groups: Joplin Citizens Against Wildwood Ranch Data Center Site
summary: The Joplin City Council approved annexation and rezoning of Wildwood Ranch for a data center. Opposition group launched referendum effort to repeal the decision and establish a 12-month data center moratorium. Two Change.org petitions gathered 1,400+ signatures. The group seeks to overturn the council's approval through a public vote.

Despite a petition with 2,500+ signatures and a 4,000-member opposition group, Hobart reached a deal with Amazon Web Services for a data center on 500+ acres. AWS plans to invest $15 billion in 2.4 GW of data center capacity across Northwest Indiana. The No Data Centers Hobart Indiana group has also launched a dedicated opposition website with legal and community action resources.

RadiusDC is planning to build two 100,000 sq ft data centers in Plainfield using 24 MW of electricity and about 700 gallons of water/day. The Plainfield Plan Commission unanimously approved the primary plat petition on Jan. 5, 2026, after more than a dozen concerned residents opposed the project during public comment. A Change.org petition opposes the project citing lack of transparency.

company: Bristolville 25 Developer LLC / BHGH Properties LLC
opposition_groups: Coalition Against Data Centers in South Ohio
summary: Lordstown was one of the first Ohio municipalities to institute a moratorium on data centers. In October 2025, developer Bristolville 25 Developer LLC submitted site plans for a 133-acre, $3.6B data center in Trumbull and Mahoning counties. The council passed a ban on data centers in November, but later rescinded it for a 180-day moratorium after legal advice. Bristolville 25 filed suit in the Ohio Supreme Court seeking a writ of mandamus to force officials to process the application. The court dismissed the case against the Village Solicitor on Dec. 24, 2025, but denied dismissal against the village. The developer filed 3,000+ pages of evidence in February 2026.

company: Ohio Industrial Realty Group (potential tenant: Amazon)
summary: A petition led by resident Cassia Core gathered nearly 2,000 signatures opposing the conversion of the historic Cleveland IX Center into an AI data center. The IX Center has hosted the Cleveland Auto Show and Christmas Connection for decades. Brook Park residents are particularly vocal about noise pollution and utility price increases.

A proposal to convert the former Pennhurst State School site in Chester County into a massive data center drew 12,000+ petition signatures. The property borders private homes, the Southeastern Veterans Center, and the Schuylkill River Trail. The Board of Supervisors scrapped the data center ordinance in December 2025, with a final vote expected in March 2026.

project_name: Cleveland Cliffs Steel Plant Data Center
summary: A developer attempted to convert the Cleveland Cliffs steel plant on 66 acres of Plymouth Township's riverfront corridor into a data center for undisclosed Big Tech tenants. Over 1,000 people signed a petition opposing the project. The application was abruptly withdrawn in November 2025 due to legal issues regarding the developer's standing.

Lackawanna County Commissioner Bill Gaughan asked Governor Shapiro and the PA General Assembly for a three-year statewide moratorium on new large-scale data center approvals. The county alone has at least 11 proposed data center campuses, six in Archbald. State Senator Rosemary Brown announced support for a moratorium and unveiled a legislative package to restrict data centers to industrial zones. State Representatives Kyle Mullins and Kyle Donahue also back the legislation.

opposition_groups: Protect Saline Township - No Data Center
summary: The $7B Stargate datacenter in Saline Township faces fierce opposition from residents and environmental groups. Over 5,000 public comments were submitted to the Michigan Public Service Commission, with the vast majority opposed. Residents call the project 'uniquely evil' for destroying wetlands, straining the grid, and reshaping their farming community. Legal challenges allege Open Meetings Act violations in the settlement that allowed the project to proceed. DTE's 1.4GW power contract was approved 3-0 despite opposition.

company: Franks Investment Company LLC / SAC III Acquisition Co
summary: Franks Investment Company LLC / SAC III Acquisition Co proposed a 2.8 million sq ft data center at the Resilient Technology Park (7340 Greenwood Road) in West Shreveport. Documents show the facility would use 7.5 million gallons of potable water per day. The Shreveport Metropolitan Planning Commission denied the special use permit 4-4 in December 2025. The City Council overturned the denial. Residents filed a lawsuit accusing the city of violating constitutional obligations, noting the operator's identity remains unknown to the public.
Augusta Township residents near Milan, Ohio created a Change.org petition to stop a proposed data center from being approved in their community.

Microsoft's cancellation of the 244-acre Caledonia data center (Project Nova) is confirmed. The decision followed a 2,000-signature petition and strong community opposition. Microsoft said it remains committed to the region and Racine County officials are working to identify an alternative site, possibly still in Caledonia. Microsoft's other WI projects (Mount Pleasant, Kenosha) are unaffected.

Residents in Fredonia and Saukville formed a joint coalition to oppose new power-line routes serving the Vantage Data Center planned for Port Washington. The 'Responsible Energy Alliance' (~500 members) and the 'Protect Fredonia Coalition' (chaired by Pastor Patti Plough) are fighting against American Transmission Company's proposed routes of 96-123 miles of new transmission lines and up to 5 new substations. ATC submitted its application to the Wisconsin Public Service Commission. The PSC will decide on routes in spring 2026.

Thomas A. Hobbs Living Trust requested rezoning of a 138-acre site along Joe Prather Highway (25025 Joe Prather Hwy, Ekron) from Agricultural/Residential to Light Industrial for data center development. After a 5-hour public hearing with 150+ residents and a 2,800-signature petition, the Planning Commission declined to recommend rezoning. Fiscal Court unanimously blocked the proposal and subsequently adopted a moratorium (Ordinance 2025-07) on new data center and IT infrastructure facility applications.

The proposed 1.8 million sq ft 'Project Loon' hyperscale data center was kept secret from the public until September 2025, despite city officials discussing initial details as early as September 2024. Hermantown changed land use plans and approved a preliminary environmental review before the public knew it was a data center. On Oct. 20, the City Council voted 4-0 to rezone 200+ acres, with nearly 300 attending and dozens testifying against. The 'Stop the Hermantown Data Center' Facebook group gained 3,500+ members. SHDC (a non-profit LLC) partnered with MCEA to appeal the city's environmental study. Residents launched lawn signs, canvassing, and door-knocking campaigns.

Red Wolf DCD Properties' $12B data center campus (six buildings, 1.8M sq ft, 600MW) on 400 acres in western Wyandotte County is on hold after a protest petition and lawsuit challenged the rezoning. Plaintiffs Neal Palmer and Haskell Farms LLC allege the Unified Government rushed review processes 'without sufficient study of short and long-term impacts.' The lawsuit also claims a commissioner was 'unduly pressured' to recuse himself and that Red Wolf orchestrated efforts to withdraw petition signatures. On October 29, a Wyandotte County district judge denied the government's motion to dismiss. Residents say elected officials have not been transparent about project impacts.

Putnam County passed a one-year moratorium on wind, solar, data centers, and small modular nuclear reactor projects in November 2025. The moratorium was intended to give leaders time to understand long-term impacts and update zoning rules. The third Indiana county (after Marshall and White) to enact a data center moratorium. The moratoriums conflict with state policy favoring tech projects and a 2019 law granting sizable tax credits for computer equipment purchases.
Residents packed council meetings to protest a zoning overlay allowing data centers. Council approved the amendment; residents appealed the decision.

Mesh Capital LLC sought to remove a protective proffer from a 57-acre property on Cabin Field Road. Supervisors voted 5-2 to deny the request in October 2025. However, since data centers have by-right use on heavy industrial zoned properties, the developer submitted three new site plans for a scaled-back 450,000 sq ft data center that staff determined to be in compliance with the proffer.

Denver-based Amp Z proposed a datacenter at the former Southland Paper Mill site near Lufkin, with 175MW of grid capacity rising to 1.1GW by 2028. A Change.org petition opposing the project received over 1,000 signatures. The Lufkin city council denied an Oncor substation permit after resident opposition. City officials emphasized the site's industrial history and zoning compatibility.

A Change.org petition opposes high-impact data center development in Rockford, IL, demanding sustainable growth alternatives. The petition calls for open dialogue, transparent cost analysis, and investment in small businesses and workforce development rather than committing local resources to energy-intensive data centers.

company: Oppidan Investment Co. / Longhorn Property Management / RR Realty Holdings
summary: Two data center developers are eyeing over 400 acres of undeveloped land in Foristell, a growing city along I-70 straddling St. Charles and Warren counties. A public hearing was delayed from Jan. 5 to Jan. 20, 2026 to find a larger venue. Resident Phyllis Elizabeth Wall brought a petition with 150+ signatures. The Change.org petition has 784 signatures. Residents cite concerns that Foristell lacks infrastructure and oversight for such development.

Project Cumulus proposes a data center near Elm Point Road and Highway 370 in St. Charles, within a protected wellhead zone endangering drinking water. The project involves storage of over 1 million gallons of diesel fuel. St. Charles moved to ban data centers for 1 year in response to community opposition.

opposition_groups: York County Citizens for Responsible Growth
summary: QTS is building a nine-building $1 billion campus near Lake Wylie, threatening the 95-acre Penland Christmas Tree Farm next door. Residents are concerned about noise, blinding security lights, and the sale of 400+ acres under the secretive code name 'Project COBRA' without community knowledge. Nearly 800 people signed a petition to stop the project. The tree farm owner worries increased heat could dry out his trees and ruin a nearly 60-year-old family business.

A regional petition opposes data center developments across Northwest Indiana, including proposals in Crown Point, Merrillville, Michigan City, Portage, Schererville, Munster, and Lowell. This reflects concern about the cumulative impact of AWS's $15 billion commitment to data center capacity in the region.

A Change.org petition demands that Kokomo prevent data centers from establishing in the city. Mayor Moore has publicly vouched to protect Kokomo from data centers, indicating strong local government support for the opposition position.
Concerned residents of South Strabane Township support a protectional and robust data center ordinance to govern any proposed mega data center development in the township.

Energy Storage Solutions plans a 'NetZero' data center in Cumberland County near three churches and many neighborhoods. Despite marketing as green energy, the facility relies on natural gas combustion. The company initially claimed 500 jobs but documents show only 69 permanent positions, mostly security and janitorial. A petition gathered 1,000+ signatures.

project_name: Tract Silver Springs / Multiple Data Centers
opposition_groups: Citizens Against Over Development in Lyon County
summary: Tract acquired 1,060 acres near Silver Springs in Lyon County to develop a datacenter park with up to 1.6GW capacity. The Lyon County Planning Commission unanimously approved the rezoning of 500+ acres. Some citizens expressed opposition citing environmental impacts and accusations of official corruption. Microsoft is also developing a 274-acre datacenter in Silver Springs. The first phase provides 150MW across 2-4 buildings.
Selectboard voted to pause conversations about a $5B data center on town-owned land after grassroots campaign by 'Protect Wiscasset.' Little info about end user or power source had been disclosed.
Board of Supervisors voted for a one-year moratorium on data center approvals in unincorporated areas, citing concerns about excessive energy and water use.

opposition_groups: Coalition Against Data Centers in South Ohio
summary: Hamilton City Council Chambers were packed on Nov. 12 with residents opposing a proposed data center requesting 240 MW of power. Resident Teri Cunningham created the Coalition Against Data Centers in South Ohio Facebook group with 600+ members. Citizens circulate petitions, create yard signs, and speak at biweekly council meetings.
Organized opposition forced withdrawal of a 2M sq ft AI data center at a retired steel mill after residents identified a legal flaw in the developer's agreement. 1,000+ petition signatures.

opposition_groups: Butte Watchdogs for Social and Environmental Justice
summary: Nine nonprofits filed a PSC complaint to stop NorthWestern Energy from shifting data center infrastructure costs to its 413,000+ residential customers. NorthWestern signed Letters of Intent with 11 data center developers for up to 1,400MW total capacity without regulatory approval. The petitioners argue that NorthWestern's plan to sign electricity service agreements before garnering regulatory approval is 'unreasonable, insufficient and contrary to Montana law' and that NorthWestern is inappropriately blocking oversight by shielding letters of intent from public review.

company: Atlas Power / Sabey Data Center Properties
opposition_groups: Butte Watchdogs for Social and Environmental Justice
summary: Sabey Data Centers proposed two 500,000 sq ft buildings (1M sq ft total) on over 600 acres at Montana Connections Industrial Park in Silver Bow County. A coalition of nine nonprofit groups warns the data centers consume massive amounts of water and electricity and could lead to much higher power bills for residents. Opposition cites noise, light pollution, water and power consumption, and potential residential electricity rate increases. Sabey entered an option agreement with the city-county in April, with a February deadline to close.

opposition_groups: Stop the Data Centers Livingston County
summary: Township enacted a unanimous six-month moratorium on Nov. 20, 2025 after ~2 hours of public comment. Trustee Bob Wilson confirmed Meta was behind the project, estimating a $9B investment and the center occupying at least 20% of the 1,000-acre space. The group 'Stop the Data Centers Livingston County' said the moratorium as passed did not apply to the current developer. In December 2025, the developers withdrew the rezoning request, stating they wanted to 'honor the current moratorium.' FracTracker Alliance celebrated the withdrawal as a community win.

After CRG withdrew its data center proposal from St. Charles, the Festus City Council unanimously annexed 240 acres for data center development. At least two residents filed lawsuits; Wake Up Jeffco organized opposition. A door-to-door petition gathered 1,400 registered voter signatures calling for a public vote on whether to allow the data center.

Serverfarm acquired 135 acres through subsidiary SF ARK1 LLC, north of the city's water treatment plant on Palmer Road. Plans call for six buildings across 135 acres totaling over 2 million sq ft. Redacted load letters reference 318MW, 307MW, and a 450MW bridge phase. The project could cost $8 billion at full build-out. Residents are calling for a pause, public hearing, and stating 'Arkansas land, water, and communities are not for sale.' A Change.org petition has been launched to stop the project.

Amazon/AWS proposed a $4B datacenter on 471 acres in Wilmington with a 30-year, 100% property tax abatement (effective 70% abatement). Jessica Sharp organized the Facebook group 'Wilmington Residents for Responsible Development' which grew to nearly 3,000 members. WRRD demands Tier 4 EPA generators, air quality monitoring, closed-loop cooling, and a Community Benefits Agreement. Daily water demand could exceed 1 million gallons. Retired executive Kurt Cooper serves as liaison to officials.
Monroe County passed a data center moratorium to review potential limitations on development.

Athens-Clarke County Commission is voting March 3 on whether to extend the current emergency moratorium (set to expire March 6). Commissioners are discussing regulations including closed-loop cooling requirements, on-site clean energy for facilities over 5MW, and potentially an outright ban on data centers. Commissioner Tiffany Taylor supports an outright ban and banning NDAs for data center proposals. Environmental groups released formal recommendations for data center rules in February.

Cloverleaf Infrastructure scrapped plans in Carlton (Kewaunee County) after local residents opposed the idea. Town Chairman David Hardtke told Cloverleaf 'if this is such a good thing for the town I want the public informational meeting and they would not do that -- it's not good for the town.' Chris Kohnle of Tisch Mills Farm Center warned 'once they take land away, it'll never come back.' The developer was subsequently also rejected in the Village of Greenleaf (Brown County). Carlton's proximity to Lake Michigan made it attractive for cooling.

opposition_groups: Economic Development Responsibility Alliance of Michigan
summary: New ordinance bans data centers in the township's airport industrial park.

Infrakey's $10B data center proposal near Elm Mott on 520 acres of farmland would be the largest industrial development in McLennan County history. The facility would include a 1.2GW gas-fired power plant larger than any existing power plant in the county. Opposition has circulated a petition with 3,000+ signatures, created a Facebook group and website, and held regular strategy meetings attended by state legislators including Rep. Pat Curry. Concerns center on air pollution from the gas plant, water consumption, noise, and health impacts. Lacy Lakeview Mayor Charles Wilson defended the project as a 'paradigm-changer' for city finances.

opposition_groups: Edgecombe Neighbors for Data Center Accountability
summary: Energy Storage Solutions proposed a $19B, 900MW data center on 300 acres of county land in Rocky Mount that would use up to 5M gallons of water/day during a severe drought. Community members wore red to the Dec 1 commissioners meeting, with nearly all speakers opposing the sale. The company claims 500 jobs but documents show only 69 permanent positions. The project may include on-site gas drilling and diesel generators, making it essentially an off-grid industrial power plant.

project_name: Project Matador / Trump Advanced Energy and Intelligence Campus
summary: Project Matador, potentially the largest data center on earth (18M sq ft on 6,000 acres), is proposed 12 miles NE of Amarillo. Fermi America entered a letter of intent with Amarillo Utilities to use 2.5-5.5 million gallons/day of potable and municipal graywater. The project threatens the Ogallala Aquifer, the region's primary groundwater supply which is being drained faster than it replenishes. Over 279 comments were filed with TCEQ, mostly opposing, plus 63 contested case hearing requests. Panhandle Taxpayers for Transparency challenged the water agreement. Some residents estimate the area has only five more years of water left. Construction was paused pending TCEQ permits, and a key tenant terminated its agreement in December 2025.

Sabey Data Centers proposed a $4B, 130-acre campus on the southwest side of Indianapolis. The Decatur Township Civic Council's executive committee unanimously voted against it. About 150 residents attended a panel with no public commenters in favor. Sabey filed for a variance of use that bypasses City-County Council vote. MDC hearing examiner recommended approval; final vote set for March 18, 2026.

opposition_groups: Kenoshans Unite Against Microsoft Data Center
summary: Microsoft purchased 240 acres in Kenosha County near I-94 and Route 142 in early 2025, with the data center scheduled to open in early 2026. The city signed an NDA with Microsoft in May 2024, keeping the project secret for six months. A resident's viral TikTok video triggered a wave of community opposition. Kenosha City Hall was standing-room only on Nov. 17 as residents packed Common Council chambers. Citizens called data centers 'parasites' and 'terrible neighbors.' A Change.org petition was created to stop the project.

Janesville signed an NDA for a proposed data center, sparking resident opposition. The group SNOW determined the data center would not reliably improve quality of life. Protests held in Janesville as part of statewide opposition. Community concerns center on secrecy deals and economic claims.

opposition_groups: Beaver Dam Education on Data Center Group
summary: Meta's $1B data center on 520 acres in Beaver Dam became controversial when it was revealed the city signed an NDA and worked with shell companies for 14 months before Meta publicly announced involvement in December 2025. The 'Beaver Dam Education on Data Center Group' is spearheading local opposition, advised by Prescott Balch, who successfully opposed Microsoft's data center in Caledonia. At least 14 residents spoke against the project at a public meeting. A lawsuit seeks release of expected energy demand from the data center. The facility is expected to open in 2027.

Gemini Capital proposed a $1.9B, 500 MW hyperscale data center on 164 acres in eastern Sioux Falls. The City Council voted 7-0 on Jan. 6, 2026 to rezone the farmland for light industrial use, despite 200+ people attending in opposition with over 5 hours of mostly negative testimony. Opponents Samantha Scarlata and Amanda Kendall formed 'Let Sioux Falls Vote' and circulated referendum petitions, but fell short with 5,012 signatures submitted vs. the required 6,704 (5% of registered voters).

project_name: Strata Expanse AI Center of Excellence
summary: Houston-based Strata Expanse hosted a ribbon cutting for an AI data center in the Haverhill area at 1476 County Road 1A. A petition gathered 5,178 signatures opposing the project. The nearby city of Ironton passed a data center moratorium on first reading. Community members demand environmental and community impact assessments before construction proceeds.

Muhlenberg Township (Pickaway County, near Circleville) passed a 12-month moratorium on new data centers after a petition gathered 700+ signatures. Key concerns include proximity to schools, potential for private wells to dry up, and impacts on the rural residential character of the township.

company: City of Newport News (Economic Development Authority)
project_name: Potential Data Center near Fort Eustis
summary: A resident created a petition after word spread about a potential data center near Fort Eustis, gathering 3,500+ signatures. Residents rallied against the proposal. However, city officials state there are no conversations, plans, or negotiations underway to bring a data center to Newport News. The petition demonstrates pre-emptive community opposition.

Infrakey proposed a $10B data center on 520 acres of farmland near Elm Mott. Opposition circulated petitions gathering 3,000+ signatures, created a Facebook group ('Stop the data center on Taylor lane') and website, and held regular strategy meetings attended by state legislators. Despite a 6-1 city council vote for a nonbinding agreement, the city has not approved annexation of the site. Concerns center on water, air pollution from gas-fired plants, and noise.

Ten Mingo County residents filed a federal lawsuit against TransGas for building two data centers and two natural gas plants without adequate community input. Despite public pushback, WV DEP approved air quality permits for the energy campus. State law HB 2014 stripped local governments of authority to regulate certified data centers, leaving residents with limited recourse.

opposition_groups: Stop the Data Center on Taylor Lane (Facebook group)
summary: Infrakey proposed a $10B data center on 520 acres near Lacy Lakeview, north of Waco — the largest industrial development in McLennan County history. The tiny city of 7,000 would gain $50M/year in tax revenue, but rural neighbors launched a fierce opposition campaign. Since November 2025, opponents have gathered 3,000+ petition signatures, created Facebook and Instagram accounts, and held regular strategy meetings. Petition organizer Mary Beechner brought concerns to the Waco City Council. Lacy Lakeview attempted to annex the site using a Texas law allowing cities to 'lasso' territory for tax revenue.
Starke County Planning Commission unanimously approved a one-year moratorium on data centers exceeding 5,000 sq ft. Fourth Indiana county to enact a data center moratorium.

IVCM is developing a $10B, 330MW AI data center on 74 acres in unincorporated Imperial County that would consume ~30% of the local utility's power. The City of Imperial filed a CEQA lawsuit; the developer counter-sued in federal court; grassroots groups NIMBY Imperial and Valle Imperial Resiste have gathered 4,000+ signatures and organized street protests.

opposition_groups: Economic Development Responsibility Alliance of Michigan
summary: Green Charter Township adopted a one-year moratorium on all data center development, citing concerns about water/energy consumption and noise.

St. Joseph County Council rejected a $13B datacenter rezoning 7-2 after a marathon 10-hour meeting, following an earlier unanimous unfavorable recommendation from the Area Plan Commission. Citizens for Responsible Economic Development led opposition focused on removal of prime farmland, water system impacts, and utility rate hikes. Amazon already has an $11B datacenter under construction nearby. A councilwoman claims her family was 'swatted' during the process.

Project Tango proposes the first hyperscale data center in Palm Beach County on 202 acres at the Central Park Commerce Center in Loxahatchee. PBA Holdings (subsidiary of Palm Beach Aggregates LLC, managed by Enrique Tomeu) and TPA Group (Atlanta-based, led by J. Bradford Smith) are the developers. The project could total 3.69 million sq ft at full build-out, including a 1.8 million sq ft AI data center. The end user (likely Amazon, Microsoft, or Google) is hidden behind the code name. Commissioners voted 7-0 to postpone the application until April 2026 for more impact studies.

Chandler City Council unanimously rejected a $2.5B AI data center. Emails opposing the project outnumbered supporters 20-to-1. Former Sen. Kyrsten Sinema lobbied for the project but the council rejected it anyway.

project_name: Bates Mill No. 3 AI Data Center
opposition_groups: Mainers Against Future AI Data Center in Lewiston
Slingshot (New England environmental health and justice organization)
summary: City Council unanimously voted down $300M AI data center. Community mobilized over a weekend to change councilors' minds within days of the proposal going public.

opposition_groups: Arizona Center for Law in the Public Interest
summary: After Tucson City Council unanimously rejected Project Blue in August 2025 due to vehement community opposition over water usage, Pima County approved the $3.6B data center 3-2. Amazon/AWS was reportedly the end user but backed out around December 2025 after Beale Infrastructure nixed water cooling in favor of air cooling (eliminating direct water consumption). The Arizona Center for Law in the Public Interest sued alleging open meeting law violations. AG Mayes also challenged the deal as unconstitutional rate-setting. Despite having no formal end user and significant opposition, the Pima County Board of Supervisors approved the new data center.

Gates County's Board of Commissioners unanimously approved a one-year moratorium on data centers in December 2025, the first NC county to do so. The ordinance prohibits accepting, processing, or approving applications for data centers, cryptocurrency mining, or other high-impact data processing facilities until December 17, 2026. Triggered by two inquiries from PACE, a firm that promotes data center development.

opposition_groups: Coalition Against Data Centers in South Ohio
summary: Waterville Township trustees unanimously approved a 12-month moratorium on data centers on Dec. 17, 2025. The city of Waterville had also voted in early December for a separate 6-month moratorium.

opposition_groups: Residents United for a Healthy Lowell
summary: Township suspended its participation after a venue was too small to accommodate opposition turnout. Franklin Lowell LLC withdrew its application.

Eagle Rock Partners proposed an 859-acre, 1,000-megawatt, $6 billion data center on Cooks Hill Road near the ACE Basin. The project would cover timberland and 200 acres of wetlands. Plans include 500 permanent jobs (30% IT, 30% facilities, 30% security, 10% admin). Coastal Conservation League and SELC have opposed the project. Landowners filed a lawsuit against Colleton County over the data center ordinance. State legislators from Colleton County also oppose the project.

opposition_groups: Maryland Data Center Reform Coalition (Nature Forward)
summary: Frederick County Council passed a 2,615-acre overlay map constraining data center development to a single zone covering less than 1% of the county's landmass.
Oshtemo Charter Township is considering a preventative moratorium on data centers, citing concerns over utility rates, water use, and environmental impact.

Panattoni Development's 'Project Cannoli' is a 1 GW data center planned for property north of I-94 between Haggerty Road and I-275 on currently vacant land. Five buildings are planned. The facility would use at least 2 million gallons of water/day. The Planning Commission voted 5-2 to grant preliminary site plan approval. 1,400 people signed a petition opposing the project, citing impacts on power grid, noise, utility costs, water use, wildlife, and property values. The township board must still approve a development agreement.

Luther residents rallied against Beltline Energy's proposed 400-acre data center, citing utility price spikes, water supply impacts, noise, and property values. The Save Oklahoma Farms and Ranches opposition group circulated a petition calling for a moratorium on data center development. The fight is part of a broader wave of data center opposition across Oklahoma.

HB 259 establishes community benefit agreement requirements between large energy use facilities (including data centers) and municipalities. Requires facility energy rates to 'fully compensate' the utility for all costs to serve the facility, preventing cost-shifting to existing consumers. SB 250 addresses requirements for renewable energy investment, infrastructure upgrades, and community benefit agreements. The Alaska Legislature also has a Joint Legislative Task Force on AI studying data center impacts.

status: pending - introduced Jan 14, 2026, assigned to Environment and Transportation Committee
summary: HB 120 prohibits construction of new data centers and prohibits state or local approvals for construction unless/until the General Assembly enacts legislation related to co-location with new or existing power generation (including natural gas, nuclear, or small modular reactors). Maryland lawmakers previously overrode Gov. Moore's veto of a data center impact study bill. The moratorium is tied to energy co-location requirements rather than a general pause.

Virginia is considering 60+ data center bills in the 2026 session. Key proposals: HB1515 would impose a temporary moratorium until July 2028 or until all existing interconnection requests are fulfilled. The Senate budget proposes phasing out the sales tax exemption (worth $1.6B/year) on Jan 1, 2027 - expedited from the original 2035 end date. A CCAN poll found 75% of voters blame data centers for rising utility costs and 9 in 10 believe lawmakers need to take action.

status: pending - referred to House Committee on Corporations
