summary: Rhode Island H 7331 (2026), introduced by House Speaker Pro Tempore Brian Patrick Kennedy (D-Hopkinton), would regulate data centers with electricity demand exceeding 50 MW. The bill requires such facilities to cover all costs associated with their electricity infrastructure, including planning, construction, expansion, operation and maintenance of generation, transmission and distribution facilities. Data centers would also be required to report daily water usage, cooling methods, and develop water conservation plans. The bill was referred to the House Committee on Corporations.

The PA Game Commission approved the controversial land swap in a 6-3 vote in late January 2026, deeding 55 acres of State Game Lands 234 plus a 200-foot right-of-way to Limerick Town Center LLC. In exchange, the commission received 176 acres in Edgemont Township (Delaware County) and 377.2 acres in Bern Township (Berks County). The Limerick Board of Supervisors voted to send a letter opposing the swap. No formal data center application has been submitted yet, but one is widely expected.

opposition_groups: STOP Data Centers & Rezoning in Conewago Twp.
summary: Residents of Conewago Township in York County oppose plans to rezone agricultural and residential land for AI data centers. The petition argues the use is incompatible with the surrounding area and demands the rezoning process halt until full community discussions take place.

Laramie County commissioners unanimously approved Project Jade, a 2.7GW data center campus (scalable to 10GW) south of Cheyenne on 1,375 acres. The $50B project by Crusoe Energy and Tallgrass Energy Partners could become the largest US data center. The Hyndman Homesites Homeowners Association wrote a letter detailing concerns about deep wells contaminating the aquifer, increased traffic and noise, light pollution, gas turbine emissions, noxious odors, and wastewater pond locations. Resident Thomas White voiced opposition about noise, light pollution, traffic, and potential water contamination from deep well drilling. The project includes 2.7GW of new natural gas-fired generation.

opposition_groups: Residents for Rural and Agricultural Land (RURAL)
summary: Applied Digital proposed a multi-billion dollar data center campus in Deuel County near Toronto (pop. ~200), with plans for two buildings at 907,000 sq ft each offering 430 MW total IT capacity. Over 100 people attended a Jan. 6, 2026 town hall to voice concerns. A volunteer firefighter worried about emergency response capacity for a facility potentially bringing 1,000+ jobs. Residents said they'd rather pay extra property taxes than have the data center. Some held meetings during harvest season, preventing agricultural residents from attending. Applied Digital stated the project is not viable without state tax incentives.

An estimated 150 residents stormed the Wood County Planning Commission meeting demanding a moratorium on data centers after Meta revealed plans for an $800 million data center on 280 acres in Middleton Township north of Bowling Green. Residents raised concerns about water consumption, electric usage, and environmental health. Residents also questioned Ohio's fast-tracked approval of a 350 MW 'Apollo' gas plant to power Meta's data center. Ohio law does not give counties the authority to issue moratoriums for specific land uses, limiting local options.
Saline City Council unanimously adopted a one-year moratorium, prompted by inquiries from Fortune 100 companies. City's zoning code did not yet define data centers.

opposition_groups: No Data Centers in Stokes County
summary: Project Delta by Engineered Land Solutions proposes an 1,800-acre AI data center campus in Walnut Cove near the Dan River, with 998 acres to be developed and the rest as floodplain. The campus would total 'several hundred megawatts' with a new dedicated substation. The county would collect an estimated $40 million in annual property taxes (current tax bill is under $8,000). Stokes County Board of Commissioners approved the rezoning 3-2 in January 2026 despite opposition from Clean Water for North Carolina and a local No Data Centers petition.
Cloverleaf Infrastructure abandoned plans in the rural Brown County village after community resistance including petitions and appeals to officials.

Madison Common Council passed a one-year moratorium on Jan 13, 2026 on new data centers and telecom centers larger than 10,000 sq ft. Separately, QTS formally withdrew its $12B data center proposal from the Village of DeForest on Jan 28, 2026 after the village determined the project was not feasible and announced its intention to deny the annexation of 1,600 acres from the Town of Vienna.

opposition_groups: GASP (Greater Birmingham Alliance to Stop Pollution)
summary: Birmingham City Council unanimously approved a 180-day moratorium on applications for new or expanding hyperscale data centers on March 3, 2026. Facilities under 20 megawatts are exempt. Two existing projects are grandfathered: DC Blox expansion and Nebius AI Factory on Lakeshore Parkway. A planning commission workshop is tentatively scheduled for March 11, followed by a public hearing March 18. New regulations could be ready for council vote by April 28.

Harvey County Commission unanimously approved a moratorium on data center construction in unincorporated areas through the end of 2028 (roughly three years). Commissioner Don Schroeder explained the moratorium gives the planning department and zoning board time to research and design regulations, as the county currently has no zoning rules for data centers, crypto mining, or related facilities. Concerns centered on water use from the Equus Beds Aquifer. Nearby Andale and Colwich residents pushed back against proposed projects, citing the importance of preserving their farming and ranching community character.

status: pending - approved for hearing by Governor
company: Project Jupiter (Dona Ana County data center development)
summary: SB 235 (Microgrid Oversight Act) requires the Public Regulation Commission to develop rules for any microgrid producing more than 20 MW, grants regulators oversight over approval and operation of microgrids, and requires proof of ability to meet state renewable energy standards for microgrids coming online after May 2026. Annual reports required after 2027 detailing power generation, water usage, and compliance. Largely driven by concerns about Project Jupiter in Dona Ana County, which proposed self-reliant 'microgrids' to power multi-billion dollar data centers.
Heated city council meeting over Project Mustang resulted in arrest of a resident for exceeding 3-minute speaking time. Hundreds signed petition opposing over water and infrastructure concerns.

Google's $9B Project Spring proposed on 827 acres of land annexed by Sand Springs. The Protect Sand Springs Alliance, led by President Kyle Schmidt, raised over $6,000 and gathered 2,058+ petition signatures. Opposition pursued multiple strategies: a nonprofit filed suit in Osage County alleging illegal annexation and ignored community input; residents launched a recall effort gathering 130+ signatures per council member in four hours; and the Osage Nation reviewed the project over concerns about impacts on tribal land. Despite opposition, the Sand Springs City Council approved the rezoning. Concerns include water use, rising utility costs, environmental impacts, noise, and threats to agricultural character.

status: pending - S.205 in committee; no current data center proposals in Vermont
summary: Vermont is considering two major data center bills. S.205, sponsored by Sen. Rebecca 'Becca' White (Windsor County), would impose a temporary moratorium on AI data centers (defined as facilities requiring >100 MW for AI workloads) through July 1, 2030, while the Public Utility Commission studies impacts on natural resources, communities, and the economy. In the House, H.727 (the Vermont Sustainable Data Centers Act) would create siting criteria for data center construction with environmental protections while minimizing ratepayer impact. The town of Royalton separately approved its own moratorium on AI and crypto data centers in March 2026.

opposition_groups: Renwick Area Against Data Centers in West Sedgwick County
summary: Sedgwick County Commissioners adopted a 90-day pause on data center permit applications (expiring April 17, 2026), with leadership staff proposing extending it to June 11, 2026. Senate majority leader Chase Blasi called for a three-year moratorium, saying 90 days isn't enough. A flood of emails and calls from residents voiced opposition to any data center development. Residents in Colwich and Andale reported hearing about data centers potentially coming less than a mile from homes. Concerns include health issues, noise, environmental impact, traffic, and heavy water use. Town halls were scheduled for March 2026. A proposed 133-mile Evergy transmission line through southern Kansas drew additional opposition from landowners.

status: pending - hearing held Feb 12, 2026, before Legislature Natural Resources Committee
summary: Nebraska LB 1111, introduced January 16, 2026 by Sen. Machaela Cavanaugh (co-sponsors: Sens. Terrell McKinney, Ashlei Spivey), would regulate data centers by requiring them to pay the full cost of new electricity infrastructure so costs are not passed to ratepayers. The bill also requires power suppliers to publicly disclose the name, developers, owners, location, proposed size, electricity demand, and contract details of data center projects. Nebraska has added 20 major data centers in the past 20 years, in part because it is the only 100% publicly powered state. The Meta data center in Sarpy County, powered through OPPD's North Omaha Station, is a key driver of the legislation. Referred to the Natural Resources Committee with a public hearing held February 12, 2026.
City Council approved moratorium after years of complaints about noise, diesel fumes from Markley facility. When fully built: 27 diesel generators, 16 cooling towers.

Kansas City Council unanimously approved zoning changes reclassifying data centers as industrial facilities, limiting them to specific zoning districts, revoking by-right status for larger data centers, and requiring City Council approval and will-serve letters from water and electric utilities.

Beale Infrastructure's $800M Project Atlas proposed 295 acres (235 + 60 acres) for an Amazon/AWS data center with 400MW capacity. The Planning Commission voted 4-1 to deny the rezoning after a 3+ hour meeting. The original plan called for 5.4 million gallons/day water usage before switching to air-cooled design. Despite the denial, the proposal goes to the Coweta city council for a final vote in April 2026.

Monterey Park City Council directed staff to explore options for a permanent data center ban citywide. The Council is expected to consider extending the 45-day moratorium at the March 4, 2026 meeting to allow additional time for staff and the City Attorney to prepare permanent prohibition options. The original moratorium was approved January 21 after a 6-hour meeting with 300+ attendees and 100+ speakers opposing a 250,000 sq ft data center by HMC in Saturn Park.

Naperville City Council voted 6-1 to deny Karis Critical's proposal for a 211,000 sq ft data center on the former Nokia/Lucent campus. A Change.org petition gathered 5,000+ signatures. Originally proposed as two data centers, the developer reduced to one after community opposition. Located near residential areas, Danada Forest Preserve, and a sensory playground for children with disabilities.

Marion County officials secretly approved a $2.4 billion data center during a winter storm, with the project listed on the agenda only as 'Project Liberty' under a nondisclosure agreement. Residents were not informed of the project details before the vote. Community backlash erupted after investigative reporting revealed the deal, with residents demanding transparency about environmental and economic impacts.

The Sembrando Esperanza Coalition, an El Paso economic and environmental justice group, called on the city, county, and utilities to terminate their contracts with Meta over a proposed $473 million natural gas power plant (the McCloud facility) to power Meta's 1GW, $1.5 billion data center on 1,000 acres in far Northeast El Paso. Organizer Veronica Carbajal said the community was told Meta would rely on renewable energy but 'when we looked at the contracts ourselves, we realized that was an illusion.' El Paso is already ranked among the worst US cities for ozone/smog and has strained water resources.

TenKey LandCo I LLC proposed a nearly $5 billion data center on approximately 200 acres near Exit 2 of I-65 at the southern end of Franklin. Plans include three 200,000 sq ft buildings ($1.6B per facility) to be developed over three years, with at least 100 jobs at $80,000 minimum salary and $17.68 million in projected tax revenue. TenKey filed a lawsuit against Simpson County in January 2026 challenging a county ordinance regulating advanced technology centers. Zoning regulators have tabled the vote multiple times.

opposition_groups: Deep South Center for Environmental Justice
East New Orleans Neighborhood Advisory Commission
summary: City Council voted unanimously (6-0) to enact a one-year moratorium on data centers, crypto mining, and server farms while working on zoning definitions.
Planning & Zoning Commission denied Corvus Investment Group's request to rezone 240+ acres to Business Park (which allows data centers) and recommended Light Industrial zoning instead.

Atlas Development filed a Development of Regional Impact for 'Project Bus,' a 12-building data center campus on approximately 350 acres in Temple (northern Carroll County). The estimated $11 billion project would generate $42 million/year in ad valorem taxes and 275 jobs. The project requires rezoning, annexation, and sewer connections. Properties span Vaughn Road, Center Point Road, Terrell Road, and Mandeville Road, north and south of I-20, about 40 miles west of downtown Atlanta.

Franklin Planning & Zoning Commission has tabled the vote on TenKey's preliminary development plan four times (most recently in a 3-1 vote). Commissioners cite the ongoing TenKey v. Simpson County lawsuit as reason to delay. The city also discovered its Planning and Zoning Administrator was never formally hired, adding procedural complications. No approval or denial vote has been taken.

opposition_groups: Wyndham Board of Directors Smart Development Task Force
summary: HHHunt withdrew its application for the 400-acre Hunting Hawk Technology Center, a 900MW data center campus with 10 buildings totaling 3.9 million sq ft on the Hunting Hawk Golf Club property near the Wyndham community. The Planning Commission voted to deny the project on Jan. 15 after hundreds of residents rallied outside the county administrative building. The proposal included electrical substations, transmission lines, and private water/sewer systems. HHHunt formally withdrew on Jan. 28. A second data center proposal on Route 33 near the Hanover-Henrico line was also introduced shortly after.

Brennan Investment Group withdrew its $2 billion data center proposal for land south of Penny Road and west of Pond Gate Drive in Barrington Hills, a suburb northwest of Chicago. The withdrawal came 'based on initial feedback' before a scheduled Feb. 2 plan commission meeting. A Change.org petition opposing the project had garnered 1,303 signatures citing concerns about air pollution, water quality, and tax impacts. The village considers the matter closed and canceled the Feb. 2 meeting.

CyrusOne (Dallas-based) proposed a 636 MW, 1.4 million sq ft data center complex on 230 acres of farmland in Talkington Township, near Waverly, ~14 miles southwest of Springfield. Investment estimated at $500M. The zoning committee voted 5-3 against a six-month moratorium, then the ZBA recommended CyrusOne receive conditional permitted use. Union members in orange were the project's most vocal supporters. Sierra Club raised concerns about lack of transparency. The Sangamon County Board will vote March 23, 2026. County Assessor Byron Deaner estimated ~$5M/year in new property taxes.

status: pending - HB 2992 and HB 3392 advanced through House committee; SB 1488 moratorium pending
summary: Multiple data center bills in Oklahoma: HB 2992 (Data Center Consumer Ratepayer Protection Act) sets guidelines for how electric suppliers address rising energy demands from data centers, requiring separate terms and a minimum 10-year service commitment. HB 3095 gives rural landowners voting power on proposed data center sites. SB 1488 places a moratorium on data centers with electrical load >100 MW until November 2029. HB 3392 requires the Oklahoma Corporation Commission to define 'Large Load Electric Customer' and report on grid impact.
SB 232 proposed one-year moratorium on hyperscale data centers (50+ MW). Senate committee voted 5-3 to table it. Sen. Howard said committee sided with industry lobbyists.

status: pending - SB 886 and SB 887 introduced January 2026, hearings upcoming
project_name: SB 978 - Data Center Community Accountability Act
summary: Sen. Steve Padilla introduced SB 886 (requiring PUC to establish special tariff to protect ratepayers from data center transmission costs) and SB 887 (allowing data centers to be eligible for Environmental Leadership Development Project certification with additional requirements). These follow failed 2025 efforts where Big Tech blocked data center regulation, leaving only a study requirement. Gov. Pritzker separately announced a two-year suspension of tax incentives (note: this is Illinois, not CA). Bills are pending hearings in the Senate.

UK-based Eneus Energy plans a 2GW, $14B data center campus on 1,785 acres near Harlingen with 16 data halls. The city-owned utility entered an agreement for 4.6M gallons/day of reclaimed wastewater for the project. Residents addressed the City Commission opposing the development, citing water and electricity concerns in the drought-prone Rio Grande Valley. Cameron County Judge Eddie Trevino said the county's authority was limited since the site is outside city boundaries. Harlingen is drafting an ordinance to cushion data center impacts as companies hunt for sprawling acreage in the area.
Carroll County is weighing a moratorium similar to those adopted by Frederick, Prince George's, Baltimore, and Montgomery counties, as data center opposition spreads across Maryland.

company: Unknown (multiple entities: Buck Canyon Properties LLC, High Table LLC, Energy Strike LLC, Hecate Energy)
opposition_groups: Adams County Residents for Responsible Development
summary: A 1,300 MW data center is proposed for the sites of two former power plants in Adams County. AES Ohio filed that the data center would start at 100 MW in 2028 and ramp up to 1,300 MW (1.3 GW) by March 2032, consuming 11.4 million megawatt hours annually -- 31 times Adams County's total electricity consumption. Residents packed a community meeting in Manchester. Alex Schaffer, a data center critic running for Adams County Commission, is organizing opposition. The Economic Development Director signed NDAs about the project.

Business owners and citizens of Urbana and Champaign County oppose a proposed data center near a new school, local nursing home, and condominium residences. Cedar Bog Nature Preserve has publicly supported the opposition effort. The petition highlights quality of life impacts including noise, air quality, and traffic concerns.

A proposed 26MW Solstice data center campus in Allen Park, Michigan has been postponed after calls for greater scrutiny. A petition gathered 1,535+ signatures, protesters rallied outside city hall, and a second petition targets non-residents in surrounding Wayne County cities. Despite a Q&A session, opposition remains fierce.

status: pending - in House Governmental Affairs Committee
summary: HB 1012 bans new data center construction from July 1, 2026 until March 1, 2027. The bill bars counties and cities from issuing any permit, license, or certificate authorizing construction of a new data center during that period, with an exception for approvals issued before July 1, 2026. Part of a wave of seven data center bills introduced in Georgia's 2026 session. Currently in House Governmental Affairs Committee.

The zoning text amendment ZTA 26-01 is advancing through the process. Public hearing held Feb. 24. The amendment would make data centers a conditional use only in industrial zones, require 500-foot setbacks from residential property, mandate Tier 4 EPA-compliant backup generators, and protect wetlands/streams. The Planning Board advanced the proposal in February. A competing bill would create a task force instead. Dickerson data center developers told they 'have a right to go forward' by county executive.

Baltimore County Council unanimously passed a bill to temporarily freeze data center development, blocking permits until the Planning Board studies potential environmental and economic impacts. The moratorium may last through end of 2026.

opposition_groups: Coalition Against Data Centers in South Ohio
summary: Maumee City Council approved a 12-month moratorium on data center construction, joining a wave of northwest Ohio communities pausing data center development while studying environmental and economic impacts.

The Ashville Village Council voted 4-2 to deny EdgeConneX's annexation request for a 490-acre parcel needed for a 680-acre, 1.8GW data center campus about 17 miles south of Columbus. EdgeConneX already owns the adjacent 200-acre parcel and planned 18 major structures including a 1.8GW substation. The village had previously passed a 180-day emergency moratorium on data centers on Dec. 20, 2025. Opposition was fierce since residents became aware of the project in November 2025. The company may still build on the smaller 200-acre site.

Mason City Council reviewed a referendum petition with 700+ signatures seeking to repeal Ordinance 266 (M-3 Technology Innovation zoning district) passed Feb. 2. Residents say the ordinance is 'dangerously inadequate' and doesn't sufficiently address lithium battery usage and noise concerns. The council is waiting on a legal opinion before deciding whether to repeal the ordinance or put it to voters. Mayor blamed 'outsiders' for the pushback, fueling further controversy.

QTS Data Centers proposed a $12B project involving five data center buildings on nearly 1,600 acres of farmland in the Town of Vienna. The 'No Data Center in DeForest' group mobilized rapidly, gaining 4,000+ Facebook followers in a village of 10,000. They printed yard signs, created an online community, and voiced opposition at numerous town and village board meetings. The DeForest Village Board voted unanimously on Feb. 3, 2026 to reject the annexation. QTS formally withdrew its application.

Diode Ventures' Gemstone Technology Park received a 3-2 approval (Mayor breaking tie) to rezone 620 acres for a data center requiring 600-800MW of power, equal to about 20% of Idaho Power's current generation capacity. Layne Thornton and Toledo Dairy filed a petition for judicial review against the city, citing traffic impacts on narrow Locust Grove Rd, power and water usage, and precedent for future farmland conversion. The Murray family warned that allowing industrial development 'sets a precedent, and soon the surrounding farmland follows.' Diode Ventures hopes to break ground in 2026 but acknowledged the timeline might be optimistic.

The Sterling Heights City Council unanimously voted to impose a one-year moratorium on any new data centers proposed in the city. Officials noted that the city's zoning restrictions, written in 1978, do not reflect modern computing infrastructure. The pause allows the planning and legal departments to study and develop new zoning regulations. Sterling Heights is part of a broader wave across southeast Michigan, where at least 27 communities now have active data center moratoria.

opposition_groups: DE-CLEAR (Delaware Data Center Coalition for Local, Environmental, and Accountable Regulation)
summary: Starwood Digital Ventures proposed 'Project Washington,' a 1.2GW datacenter on 580 acres near Delaware City. The facility would consume as much power as ~1 million homes, nearly twice all housing units in Delaware. Consumer advocates predict power bills could rise $70/month. Delaware's DNREC denied the Coastal Zone permit, ruling the project is 'heavy industry not compatible with the Coastal Zone.' Starwood is appealing. Dozens of residents pushed back at a tense town hall.

Delaware's DNREC denied Starwood Digital Ventures' application for a 6-million-sq-ft data center near Delaware City under the Coastal Zone Act, citing 516 backup diesel generators storing 2.5 million gallons of diesel as heavy industry. The 1.2 GW facility could consume as much power as 1 million homes. Starwood appealed on Feb 25, 2026.

company: Trammell Crow Company (White Oak), Pumpkin Technology Campus developer (unnamed)
opposition_groups: Citizens for Open Governance, Columbia County Georgia
summary: Columbia County Board of Commissioners approved the White Oak (Trammell Crow) and Pumpkin Center data center rezonings in early February 2026, rejecting a third proposal. Citizen Gregory P. Guido Jr. filed two judicial review petitions on Feb 25 challenging both approvals, alleging procedural defects, incomplete water/sewer analysis, outdated traffic study, and conflicts with the county comprehensive plan.

opposition_groups: Mt. Orab Residents for Responsible Development
summary: Village Councilman Eric Lang introduced two ordinances for a 180-day data center moratorium amid community outcry over a mysterious 1,000-acre 'mega site' project requesting up to 2,000 MW. A Facebook group grew to 1,700+ members and nearly 1,000 residents signed a petition. Council members had signed NDAs about the project.

New York S.9144 (Senate, Sen. Liz Krueger, co-prime sponsor Sen. Kristen Gonzalez) / A.10141 (Assembly, Asm. Anna Kelles), introduced February 6, 2026, would impose a moratorium of at least 3 years and 90 days on permits for new data centers designed to use 20+ MW. During the moratorium, the DEC must issue a generic environmental impact statement covering energy, water, air quality, GHG emissions, and e-waste; the PSC must assess rate impacts and protect residential customers from cost increases. Would not affect the public Empire AI research initiative in Buffalo. Described as the 'strongest-in-the-nation' data center moratorium. Additionally, S.6394 (the NY Sustainable Data Centers Act) requires data centers to source 1/3 renewable energy by 2030, 2/3 by 2035, and 100% by 2040. S.8540 (the AC/DC Act) addresses rate classification and cost responsibility for data center operators. Over 100 community, environmental, and faith organizations called for passage of S.9144/A.10141 in a March 2026 letter to Gov. Hochul.

State Sen. Liz Krueger and Assemblymember Anna Kelles introduced S.9144, a three-year moratorium on new data center permitting while DEC completes an environmental impact statement. Over 100 New York organizations endorsed the bill, called the 'strongest data center moratorium in the country.'

Bipartisan bill HB 609 would limit sales tax exemptions to data center-specific equipment (excluding construction materials), sunset the exemption in 2036, and require data centers built after March 2026 to pay power rates that fully cover utility costs. Rep. Mickelsen separately introduced a bill to prevent the Idaho PUC from approving any new customer requiring 20+ MW without proving it would not raise rates for other customers. Multiple bills addressing tax breaks, water usage reporting, and rate protection.

status: pending - HB 706 introduced; Senate bills being introduced
summary: HB 706 bans utilities from recovering data center infrastructure costs by shifting them onto other customers and requires long-term service agreements of at least 12 years. Mirrors AEP's existing tariff requiring data centers to cover 85% of energy costs. Senate Democrats introduced additional bills to eliminate tax breaks, require developers to cover infrastructure costs, and impose greater oversight. Around 18 municipalities have enacted local moratoriums on data centers.

opposition_groups: No Data Centers in the Driftless
summary: An unnamed company is seeking 400-500 acres in Cassville for a $1B data center. The 'No Data Centers in the Driftless' grassroots campaign was organized by Lemon Door restaurant owner Melodie Betts and led by Mount Hope resident Pete Moris. About 20 concerned citizens met at Lemon Door in Fennimore on Jan. 5, 2026 for an informational meeting. The group plans a webpage, Facebook page, and additional informational meetings. The Grant County Economic Development Corp. expects to learn by spring 2026 whether the proposal advances.

Montour County commissioners unanimously denied Talen Energy's rezoning of 800+ acres from agricultural to industrial for a datacenter and power station. The Concerned Citizens of Montour County Facebook group amassed 2,300 followers after starting with just 4 members in August 2025. Their first town hall drew 120 people, and a petition gathered 3,000 signatures -- twice the township's population. The group retained a zoning/land use lawyer. Opposition was fueled by proximity to a nature preserve and residential area.

Hood County (40 miles SW of Fort Worth) has received at least five data center applications. Commissioners voted down a proposed moratorium 3-2, twice, despite 8 hours of public testimony. The 2,100-acre Comanche Circle project sits in a recharge zone for the Upper Trinity Aquifer and could use 1M gallons/day. State Sen. Paul Bettencourt sent a letter to AG Paxton warning that counties lack constitutional authority to impose moratoriums, chilling local opposition. Residents are still adjusting to a nearby bitcoin mining operation in Granbury where neighbors report constant noise affecting people, pets, and wildlife.

opposition_groups: Stop the AI Data Center in Independence
summary: Dutch-based Nebius plans a hyperscale data center scaling to 1.2 GW -- 18 times the electricity of all 55,000 Independence households combined. The 'Stop the AI Data Center in Independence' Facebook group, co-founded by resident Rachel Gonzalez, grew to 2,500+ members within weeks. Over 200 residents packed a February 10 meeting. Despite overwhelming opposition, the Independence City Council voted on March 2, 2026 to approve the project, issuing $150 billion in industrial revenue bonds. Nebius received a 90% personal property tax abatement and will not pay real property taxes. A Change.org petition was also created. Nebius is projected to pay $651M in PILOT fees over 20 years.

company: Unknown (inquiries from out-of-town companies for paper mill site)
summary: Board of Aldermen unanimously passed a 12-month moratorium after ~50 residents spoke out. Prompted by inquiries about using the decommissioned paper mill site for a data center.

status: defeated - bill lapsed/died March 3, 2026 after industry pushback; sponsor committed to reintroduce next session
summary: HB 2515 would have required data centers to pay additional utility charges, comply with clean energy requirements (80% clean power by 2030, 100% by 2045), publish annual reports on water/energy/pollution, and shut off power at peak demand times. Microsoft publicly labeled the proposed regulations 'uniquely anti-competitive.' The bill died when it failed to advance before the session deadline on March 3, 2026. Sponsor Rep. Doglio committed to continuing work on the legislation next session.

status: pending - HB 1265 in Commerce and Consumer Affairs Committee; SB 439 passed Senate and moved to House; HB 1739 recommended to be killed by committee
summary: Four data center bills in NH: HB 1265 would impose a one-year moratorium on data center construction while a legislative study examines environmental effects. SB 439 enables local planning boards to regulate data centers (passed Senate). HB 1739 would establish tax incentives and grid modernization requirements (recommended to be killed). HB 1724 would require utilities to report on costs and capacity related to data centers.

B+F Timpanogos proposed converting a warehouse in Provo's East Bay into a 66,000 sq ft, 30MW data center on 9 acres. Mayor Marsha Judkins formally opposed the project, saying an additional data center would not be in residents' best interest. Despite the mayor's opposition, the Planning Commission recommended rezoning approval 5-2 and unanimously approved the project plan. Concerns focused on water usage, the facility's proximity to Rocky Mountain University, and whether approximately 30 jobs would justify the water and power consumption.

QTS Data Centers plans a $2 billion data center at 2883 Gordon Highway that could be twice the size of Augusta Mall. The company filed to expand to six two-story buildings totaling over 2.15 million sq ft. The project is expected to create 160-220 jobs and could be completed by Q2 2026. The project flew 'under the radar' until local news coverage revealed its scale, surprising neighbors.

status: pending - PSC reviewing rate proposals from Wisconsin Electric Power Company and Alliant Energy
company: Microsoft (Mount Pleasant, $13.3B project), Meta (Beaver Dam campus via Alliant Energy)
summary: Two Wisconsin PSC dockets address data center rate structures. Docket 6630-TE-113: We Energies proposed a 'Very Large Customer' rate class for customers with electricity demands above 500 MW (targeting hyperscale data centers like Microsoft in Mount Pleasant). Over 200 people joined virtual hearings on Feb 10-11, 2026, with ~85% of speakers opposing the proposal, urging that tech companies pay their full share of infrastructure costs. Critics argue the proposal would only require data centers to pay 75% of infrastructure costs, leaving 25% subsidized by the public. Docket 6680-TE-115: Alliant Energy's Individual Contract Rate Agreement to serve a data center campus in Beaver Dam (widely assumed to be Meta). The PSC required Alliant to refile the contract with fewer redactions after advocates raised transparency concerns. A coalition of 42 environmental justice, faith, conservation, and community organizations submitted formal comments opposing the proposals.

status: pending - moratorium amendment proposed Feb 12, committee work sessions ongoing
company: LiquidCool (seeking exemption for Loring data center)
summary: Maine LD 307 ('Resolve, to Establish the Maine Artificial Intelligence Data Center Coordination Council'), sponsored by Rep. Melanie Sachs (D-Freeport), would establish a data center coordination council within the energy resources department to evaluate policy options for data center development. During a February 12, 2026 work session, Sachs proposed a moratorium amendment to pause building, permitting, and establishment of data centers with loads above 20 MW until July 1, 2028. The amendment was opposed by Sen. Matt Harrington (R-York), who said it would harm a data center development in his district. The LiquidCool Solutions AI data center at the former Loring Air Force Base (2 MW, using patented immersion-cooling technology, no water for cooling) would likely not be affected by the moratorium; LiquidCool expects a waiver since the facility is 26 MW total and uses existing underutilized power generation.

status: pending - two competing bills in committee, SB 26-102 (regulation) vs HB 26-1030 (incentives)
summary: Two competing approaches: SB 26-102 requires data centers to pay full electricity and grid investment costs, obtain power from renewables (100% by 2031), optimize water efficiency with transparent reporting, and engage with disproportionately impacted communities. HB 26-1030 creates a voluntary incentive program with 20-year, 100% sales/use tax exemption for data centers meeting investment ($250M minimum), labor, and environmental conditions. Environmental groups back SB 26-102 while tech industry supports HB 26-1030.

Blanchester Village Council unanimously approved a moratorium on data center development via Ordinance 2026.007, citing concerns about being 'circled' by nearby data center proposals in Wilmington and Mt. Orab.

Metrobloks proposed an urban data center on 14 acres at the former Sherman Drive-In site. Residents organized protests citing environmental justice concerns, as research shows Black communities near data centers report worsened air quality. Hearing examiner approved despite opposition; the neighborhood plans to appeal.

status: pending - introduced Feb 26, 2026, referred to Committee on Utilities, Technology and Tourism
company: Microsoft (Mount Pleasant data center), Meta (Beaver Dam data center)
summary: SB 1061 would ban data center construction until the state establishes a data center planning authority, prohibits energy/water cost-shifting to residents, creates a land and community funding mechanism, eliminates subsidies, mandates public reporting, creates pollution regulations, requires 100% renewable energy, requires prevailing wages, restores PSC planning authority, prohibits NDAs between data centers and government, and creates enforcement/penalty structures. Intent is not permanent prohibition but responsible development.

opposition_groups: Walla Walla chapter, Democratic Socialists of America
summary: The Port of Walla Walla Commissioners unanimously approved selling 553 acres to Amazon Web Services for a $4.8 billion data center campus with 16 warehouses spanning 3.4 million sq ft at the Wallula Gap Business Park. Local opponents, including the College Place Mayor, argued the project threatens the valley's agricultural roots, limited water supplies, and natural beauty. Critics cited water consumption as 'ecologically devastating' in dry Eastern Washington and noted elevated nitrate levels in well water near an existing data center in Boardman. Washington HB 2515 is advancing through the Senate to regulate data center water and energy impacts.

Chatham County approved a 12-month moratorium on the development of new data centers and cryptocurrency mining facilities on February 13, 2026. The ban applies to all development approvals for data centers, data processing facilities, cryptocurrency mining operations and 'any uses associated with data processing facilities,' including web services, hosting, and genome sequencing. The moratorium applies to unincorporated areas and lasts through February 2027.

Preserving Montgomery County LLC (formed Dec. 27, 2025 by Sabrina Cope of Truxton) filed a lawsuit in Cole County Circuit Court alleging the commission violated Missouri open meetings and open records laws (Sunshine Law violations). The suit claims the county concealed negotiations, improperly approved up to $35B in industrial revenue bonds, and gave inadequate public notice. The project, dubbed 'Project Green,' is a 1,000-acre AWS data center campus in New Florence, filed via NorthPoint Development LLC. The commission approved the tax incentive framework on Dec. 19, 2025. Scores of residents packed meetings to oppose.

company: Oppidan Investment Co. / Connect Data Centers
opposition_groups: Coalition for Responsible Data Center Development
summary: First Minnesota city to enact a data center moratorium. One-year pause on facilities using more than 20 MW or within 500 feet of residential land.

San Marcos City Council voted 5-2 at 2:14 AM on Feb. 17 to deny zoning amendments for a $1.5B, 380MW data center by Highlander SM One LLC (Fort Worth) on 200 acres on Francis Harris Lane. Over 100 residents spoke against it during 8+ hours of testimony. The overflow room roared with cheers when the denial vote came. Part of a broader fight in Hays County where 5 data centers have been proposed amid historic drought conditions.

DC BLOX is the company behind the Lowndes County data center proposal. DC BLOX's CEO informed the landowner (Pope Langdale) the facility could create up to 400 jobs with $135,000 average salary and would use closed-loop cooling. Lowndes Citizens Against Data Centers (LCAD), organized with Alpha Kappa Alpha Upsilon and the Valdosta State University Anthropology Club, hosted a town hall where citizens pushed back on the potential development. In fall 2025, a parcel was rezoned for the potential data center.
Richfield Township Board of Trustees passed a data center moratorium on Feb. 17, joining other northwest Ohio communities in pausing development while studying impacts.

status: pending - co-sponsorship memos circulated, bills not yet formally introduced
summary: Sen. Katie Muth proposed a three-year moratorium on hyperscale data center construction including associated power generation and transmission infrastructure. Sen. Rosemary Brown (R) also supports a moratorium and unveiled a legislative package restricting data centers to industrial zones. Rep. Jamie Walsh (R) plans a separate regulatory package. Lackawanna County Commissioner Bill Gaughan urged Gov. Shapiro to support calls for a three-year moratorium. Bipartisan opposition with over 50 planned data centers facing resistance from community groups across the state.

company: CloudBurst Data Centers / Highlander SM One LLC
summary: Hays County water advocates identified five potential data center developments and organized protests. A proposed 30-day moratorium on water-intensive building permits was tabled after county attorneys warned it would trigger lawsuits. San Marcos City Council voted 5-2 to deny a $1.5B data center after 8+ hours of public testimony with 100+ residents opposing. Aquifers in the region are at historic lows.

xAI purchased a power plant in Southaven and plans to invest $20B+ in data centers in DeSoto County (Colossus 2). The company installed 27 unpermitted gas turbines capable of generating 495MW — equivalent to a conventional power plant — without obtaining any permits. The turbines roar 'like jet engines' day and night, causing respiratory problems and insomnia for residents. The Safe and Sound Coalition gathered 900+ signatures demanding shutdown. At a Feb 2026 public hearing packed with hundreds of attendees, not a single person spoke in favor of xAI's proposed permanent air permit. Earthjustice and the SELC, on behalf of the NAACP, sent a 60-day notice of intent to sue for Clean Air Act violations. The facility would likely be the largest industrial source of NOx in the 11-county Memphis metropolitan area. Governor Tate Reeves called it the largest private investment in Mississippi history.
Monclova Township trustees approved a 12-month moratorium on data centers on Feb. 18, joining Maumee, Waterville, and Richfield Township in northwest Ohio's wave of data center pauses.

opposition_groups: NJ Build Up Resistance Now (NJ B.U.R.N.)
summary: New Brunswick City Council voted unanimously on Feb 19, 2026 to remove data centers as a permitted use from the Jersey-Sandford Redevelopment Plan, killing the Amzak Capital-backed 27,000 sq ft AI data center proposal. The site at 100 Jersey Avenue was restored to its original designation as public park space. The decision came after hundreds of residents rallied inside and outside City Hall in opposition.

status: pending - advanced past first funnel deadline
summary: Iowa HF 2447, proposed by Rep. Cindy Golding (R-Cedar Rapids), creates new reporting requirements and utility rate structures for data centers. Data centers must file quarterly reports with the Iowa DNR on water usage (source, volume, efficiency) and quarterly energy reports with the Iowa Utilities Commission (power consumption, on-site generators, sustainability metrics like waste heat reuse). All data must be publicly shared online within 30 days. The bill also requires the Iowa Utilities Commission to create a separate rate class for data centers to prevent grid upgrade costs from being shifted to residents. Separately, HF 2261 would create a new water utility customer class for energy facilities requiring 20+ MW. The bill survived legislative funnel week in February 2026.

Over 1,700 residents signed a petition opposing a potential data center on 500+ acres of prime farmland near Shaker Village at Pleasant Hill. Hundreds packed the Mercer County Fairgrounds to voice unified opposition. The land is close to KU's E.W. Brown power plant and the Kentucky River. No formal application has been submitted but the county hired a consultant to evaluate data center regulations.

status: defeated - passed State Affairs Committee 5-3, but failed (tabled) in full Senate vote
summary: SB 232 would have imposed a one-year moratorium on the construction or expansion of hyperscale data centers in South Dakota. The bill passed the State Affairs Committee 5-3 but was ultimately killed during the full Senate vote. Part of a broader set of data center legislation in South Dakota's 2026 session.

The City of Aurora is developing new data center regulations as its existing moratorium nears expiration. The proposed rules include maximum noise levels of 59 dB daytime and 49 dB nighttime, a ban on roof-mounted chiller units within 1,500 feet of residential/educational/hospital uses, and ground-mounted chillers and generators at least 1,000 feet from such uses. The regulations stem from extensive noise complaints by residents near the existing CyrusOne data center. Over 200 residents voiced concerns at an open house on Feb. 12 and at a Planning and Zoning Commission meeting. CyrusOne's permanent sound solution is due by Sept. 30, 2026.

status: pending - POWER Act introduced Feb 11, 2026; Governor Pritzker announced two-year suspension of data center tax incentives effective July 1, 2026
summary: The POWER Act (SB 4103/HB 5513) establishes comprehensive statewide standards for data center development including clean energy procurement requirements, utility cost responsibility, water use reporting, pollution controls, and community engagement. Requires data centers to submit clean energy supply plans and establishes new rate tariffs for hyperscale data centers. Governor Pritzker separately announced a two-year suspension of state tax incentives for new data center developments effective July 1, 2026. Data Center Coalition called the bill potentially harmful to the market.

status: pending - moratorium legislation announced but formal bill number not yet assigned
opposition_groups: Coalition for Responsible Data Center Planning
summary: Multiple Minnesota data center bills are in play. SF 608 (94th Legislature, 2025-2026), co-sponsored by Sens. Lieske, Maye Quade, Wesenberg, Gruenhagen, and McEwen (companion bill HF 1109), would prohibit 'enterprise data centers' (800,000+ sq ft) in certain districts. Sen. Jen McEwen (DFL-Duluth) announced she will introduce a separate bill imposing a two-year moratorium on new data center permits while the state studies long-term effects. Sen. Erin Maye Quade (DFL-Apple Valley) plans legislation to prohibit NDAs for data centers and end tax exemptions for big tech. Previously, HF 16 (2025 special session, sponsored by Rep. Greg Davids and Sen. Ann Rest) passed and was signed into law (effective June 15, 2025), establishing initial data center regulations. On February 18-20, 2026, a large rally at the state Capitol drew hundreds of opponents calling for a statewide moratorium.

Dryden Town Board unanimously banned data centers and cryptocurrency mines, becoming the first town in New York State to outlaw data centers. The ban followed an 18-month land-use moratorium and was driven by concerns over energy draw, water use, and noise.

Community members held a massive public meeting regarding the CoreSite DE3 data center being built in the Globeville/Elyria-Swansea neighborhood. CoreSite pulled out of the meeting at the last minute citing safety concerns, enraging hundreds of neighbors who showed up anyway. The overflow rooms were completely full with the outside crowd pounding on windows. The GES Coalition demands transparency around power/water use, environmental impact study, disclosure around diesel backup generator use, and high quality generators to limit emissions.

Mayor Mike Johnston announced a one-year moratorium on new data center construction. The city will review zoning, water, energy use, and affordability impacts amid opposition in Globeville-Elyria-Swansea.

opposition_groups: Lincoln County residents (Commissioner Joel Arends supporters)
summary: Lincoln County Commissioner proposed a 1-year moratorium on hyperscale data centers requiring 100+ MW, but no commissioner seconded the motion, killing the measure. The commission voted 3-2 to refer regulations to Planning and Zoning.

The public hearing on Amazon's $2B data center rezoning was postponed due to a paperwork error (duplicated parcel numbers). The Planning and Zoning Commission hearing is now rescheduled for March 23, 2026. Community opposition remains strong, with dozens of residents packing prior meetings to oppose the 330+ acre industrial rezoning. No decision has been made yet.

Cobb County Board of Commissioners voted unanimously to implement a 180-day moratorium on new data center permits and rezoning applications in unincorporated areas. Resolution cited noise pollution, air quality impacts from cooling systems and backup generators, aesthetics concerns, and significant effects on power and water utilities.

Franklin County Commissioners voted unanimously to maintain a year-long data center moratorium preventing applications until February 24, 2027. The moratorium gives the county time to gather information and develop a new zoning code for data center infrastructure.

status: mixed - HB 1005 (50-year tax break) defeated 9-3 in committee; SB 232 (moratorium) killed; SB 135 (bill of rights) passed committee 5-4
summary: Multiple data center bills saw different outcomes: HB 1005 (50-year sales tax exemption for data centers) was defeated 9-3 in House State Affairs Committee. SB 232 (one-year moratorium) passed committee but was killed in full Senate vote. SB 135 ('Data Center Bill of Rights for Citizens' from Sen. Karr) passed committee 5-4 - it would set protections for utility ratepayers and resources while banning tax exemptions for data centers. Bills addressing noise levels, minimum distances from residences, and water/electricity regulation were also halted.

status: stalled - HB 1245 never advanced beyond first reading; local moratoriums advancing instead
company: Meta (Lebanon data center), Amazon ($11B Lake Michigan data center), Google ($2B Fort Wayne campus), Sabey (Decatur Township proposal)
summary: Over 200 people from 40 counties attended a 'People's Hearing on Data Centers' at the Indiana State Library on Feb 24, organized by Hoosier Action. Attendees delivered postcards urging legislators and Gov. Braun to regulate data centers. Rep. Shackleford's HB 1245 (requiring IURC to study data center energy impacts) never advanced. Citizens Action Coalition has called for a statewide moratorium since October 2025. Local moratoriums are advancing: Fulton County approved a one-year moratorium on March 3; Starke County and Dearborn County also enacted moratoriums.
Portage enacted a temporary moratorium on data centers and battery storage facilities through end of year.

Atlanta-based Beltline Energy pitched a $16B, 16-building datacenter campus on 500 acres requiring annexation into Pacific. Hundreds of residents packed Pacific High School in opposition. The Missouri Department of Conservation flagged impacts on bald eagles, bats, eastern hellbender, fish, mussels, and plants. Residents fear well water depletion from estimated 1M gallons/day cooling needs. Beltline withdrew its rezoning request minutes into a special meeting, citing incomplete funding agreements. Community groups hired an environmental attorney.

The GES Coalition delivered a 'Good Neighbor Proposal' to CoreSite opposing their DE3 expansion in Elyria-Swansea, one of Denver's most environmentally burdened neighborhoods. Over 300 individuals and 11 organizations signed demanding transparency on energy/water use.

Phillipsburg Town Council voted unanimously to approve first reading of an ordinance banning data centers in all zones, citing water and energy demands. Final vote and public hearing scheduled for March 10, 2026. Officials referenced the town's Master Plan prioritizing rural character and agricultural heritage.

Dearborn County Board of Commissioners approved a one-year moratorium on rezoning applications for data centers, commercial solar, and battery storage facilities. Over 1,700 people signed an online petition calling for the moratorium. Planning commission will begin considering rule changes in April.

company: Trammell Crow Company (White Oak), Pumpkin Technology Campus developer (unnamed)
project_name: White Oak Business Park / Pumpkin Center
summary: Citizen filed two judicial review petitions on Feb. 25 challenging Columbia County Board of Commissioners' approval of data center rezonings for White Oak Business Park and Pumpkin Center projects. Petitions allege procedural defects, incomplete Development of Regional Impact review, failure to notify school district, and lack of verified water/sewer capacity analysis.

Opposition escalating: (1) Mooringsport Mayor and residents filed lawsuit in Caddo District Court challenging Shreveport's data center permit for TXLAAR LLC, alleging NDA violations and lack of transparency; Sierra Club joining as co-plaintiffs. (2) North Caddo Parish communities organized boycott of March 3 data center meeting in Blanchard. (3) State Rep. Danny McCormick demanding transparency on NDA agreements signed by local officials. (4) Caddo Commission declined to vote on Commissioner Kracman's proposed one-year water moratorium for Caddo Lake. (5) Amazon announced $12B investment for NW Louisiana data centers, promising to limit water use.

company: T5 Data Centers (existing); second developer proposed
summary: City Council approved a six-month moratorium 5-2 to study impacts on water, energy, design standards, setbacks, and noise.

status: pending - referred to House Committee on Government Operations; likely veto by Gov. Whitmer
summary: Bipartisan House Bills 5594-5596 would create the 'Data Center Regulation Act' and halt construction of new data centers in Michigan until April 1, 2027. The moratorium would give local communities time to assess project ramifications. At least 25 Michigan communities have enacted local data center moratoriums. Gov. Whitmer opposes the bills, calling any legislation preventing economic growth 'an automatic non-starter' that would be vetoed.

status: advancing - SB 484 passed Florida Senate unanimously
summary: SB 484 establishes a comprehensive framework for regulating large-scale data centers (50+ MW peak load), requiring PSC to adopt baseline tariff and service requirements, preserving local government control over planning. Passed Senate unanimously. HB 1007 is more restrictive, prohibiting hyperscale data centers (25+ MW) on environmentally sensitive lands, imposing siting prohibitions, water permitting restrictions, and banning economic incentives. Multiple bills reflect DeSantis-backed data center restrictions.

status: pending - introduced in U.S. Senate, reception unclear
summary: The GRID Act is a bipartisan federal bill requiring new data centers with 20+ MW power demand to find their own power sources outside of the electric grid and report estimated utility usage for five years. Existing facilities must cover costs that would otherwise be passed to customers. Violators face $1 million/day civil fines. Also requires project labor agreements. First bipartisan federal bill addressing data center impacts on utility bills. State-level: SB 245 would revoke CT data center tax incentives.

Penzance Management announced a $4B data center in Berkeley County, the first certified 'high-impact' project under WV's HB 2014. The project is exempt from local zoning, noise, lighting, and land use ordinances. Residents and advocacy groups raised concerns that long-term environmental costs outweigh short-term revenue, and that the law denies communities any voice in siting decisions. Lawmakers blocked multiple amendments to restore local control or require water usage reporting.

status: pending - filed but not yet assigned to committee
summary: Missouri has multiple data center bills. HB 3362 and HB 3364 (Reps. Colin Wellenkamp, Scott Miller, Mike Costlow), titled the 'AI Infrastructure, Grid Integrity and Water Resource Protection Act', would require large-load consumers to pay the full cost of electricity and infrastructure (including grid upgrades and new generation capacity) and create a water permit requirement from the DNR for facilities using 2+ million gallons of fresh water per day or 80% of local available capacity. Such facilities would need to build new freshwater capacity or create closed-loop freshwater systems. Separately, SB 1396 (Sen. Mike Henderson, R-Desloge) would alter 2018 economic development incentives to prevent data centers from taking advantage of energy incentive programs that were not designed for them.

Spring Hill City Council voted on October 23, 2025 to move forward with rezoning 316 acres from Rural Residential to M-1 Industrial for a potential data center, though no end user or specific business has been identified. About 300 homes would be impacted, and Spring Hill High School is only 3/4 mile from the proposed site. Approximately 45 people were originally notified about the rezoning. A public hearing was rescheduled to March 25, 2026. Residents are organizing to fight the rezoning, raising concerns about noise, traffic, property values, and proximity to schools.

status: pending - referred to House Committee on Government Operations; Gov. Whitmer has signaled veto
summary: Same as michigan-statewide-mi-2026-02-26. Bipartisan moratorium bills would halt all data center construction until April 2027. Lansing Regional Chamber of Commerce opposes the moratorium. The bills were introduced on a day of statewide action. At least 25 communities have active local moratoria on data centers in Michigan.

status: pending - in House Committee on Government Operations; Gov. Whitmer threatened veto
summary: Bipartisan group of Michigan House lawmakers introduced a three-bill package to temporarily halt all new data center development statewide until April 1, 2027. At least 27 Michigan communities now have active local moratoria in place while drafting zoning rules. Bills would prevent state EGLE and local governments from issuing data center permits.

The Missouri AI Infrastructure, Grid Integrity and Water Resource Protection Act is filed as HB 3362 and HB 3364 by Reps. Colin Wellenkamp, Scott Miller, and Mike Costlow (R-Dardenne Prairie). The legislation would establish separate utility rate structures for large customers with 50+ MW of annual peak demand, and create a permit system for large water users requiring facilities withdrawing 2+ million gallons/day to obtain 5-year permits from the DNR. If water use exceeds 80% of available local capacity, the facility must build out new freshwater capacity. The Missouri Future Caucus has prioritized this legislation. The bills had not yet been sent to committee as of late February 2026.

TigerDC formally withdrew its $3 billion Project Spero data center proposal from Spartanburg County after the county council voted not to offer a tax incentive following weeks of strong public opposition. Hundreds of residents showed up at a public hearing against the project, and thousands signed a petition. The 'No Data Centers in Spartanburg' Facebook page organized community opposition. County council stated it would not vote on any new data center projects until the state legislature establishes statewide rules.

The Henrico Board of Zoning Appeals unanimously denied Centra Logistics' vested-rights appeal to build a 1 million sq ft data center complex on Darbytown Road in Varina. The BZA upheld the December 2025 decision by the county Planning Director, finding Centra had not received a 'significant affirmative governmental act,' was not the landowner when it filed its claim, and had not obtained the provisional-use permit now required under Henrico's June 2025 ordinance. Residents of the nearby New Market Village subdivision strongly opposed the project. Centra may appeal to Henrico Circuit Court.

Scranton Materials LLC filed an appeal after the Ransom Township Board of Supervisors rejected its zoning amendment application that would have permitted data centers at 819 Newton Road. The company argues the township's zoning ordinance unlawfully excludes data centers, claiming Pennsylvania municipalities must allow every type of lawful land use somewhere within their borders. The proposal drew dozens of residents and elected officials who opposed the plans at multiple public hearings. The township's existing noise and building height requirements were cited as too restrictive for data centers.

Marana residents filed a lawsuit against the Town of Marana after the Town Clerk rejected referendum petitions with over 2,800 signatures that sought to force a public vote on the town council's Jan. 6 unanimous rezoning approval for Amazon's Project Blue data center on two 300-acre parcels. The petitions were invalidated on a technicality -- they omitted legal property descriptions required for zoning ordinances. A Pima County judge heard arguments on March 4. Three residents also launched town council campaigns in response. Separately, the No Desert Data Center Coalition filed suit against Pima County for approving a related land sale and rezoning.

Linn County passed a new ordinance requiring data center builders to conduct environmental reviews, limit light pollution and noise, and compensate the county for infrastructure costs. This was triggered by Google informing the county in October 2025 of plans for a six-building campus in Palo alongside the Duane Arnold Energy Center (Iowa's sole nuclear plant). Residents at public hearings raised concerns about water use, electricity rates, light pollution, and low-frequency noise impacts on livestock. Some called for a complete moratorium.

Mason County planning commission approved an ordinance paving the way for a 2,000-acre data center by an undisclosed company, despite significant opposition from residents and the We Are Mason County Ky. grassroots group. The ordinance requires 750-foot setbacks from residences, limits noise to 50 dB, and restricts construction to daytime hours. Opponents cite loss of prime farmland and secretive NDA-protected planning.

Linn County Board of Supervisors unanimously approved one of the most comprehensive local data center zoning ordinances in the country on Feb. 18, 2026. Creates a new data-center-specific zoning district with requirements including comprehensive water study, water-use agreement before construction, 1,000-foot setbacks from residential zones, noise and light pollution limits, road damage compensation, and community betterment fund contributions. County planning director says no ordinance in Iowa asks for more requirements.

SELC and Sierra Club filed lawsuit challenging Georgia EPD's air permit allowing Georgia Power to add four gas- and oil-fired turbines at Plant Bowen to serve data center power demand. Environmental groups argue the new units would release smog threatening air quality for millions of Atlanta-area residents. The case was filed before Georgia Office of State Administrative Hearings.

company: Cumulus Real Estate Holdings LLC (Talen Energy subsidiary)
summary: Rezoning request to allow data centers on 460 acres of rural land in Hollenback Township was deemed denied and is now heading to Luzerne County Court. Residents packed meetings to demand answers about data center impacts. Landowners were reportedly offered $175K per acre, causing a divisive community split.

Over 100 New York groups called for passage of the statewide three-year data center moratorium bill, the 'strongest-in-the-nation' proposal that would prevent construction of new data centers while state agencies study environmental and economic impacts. Food & Water Watch coordinated the coalition effort.

opposition_groups: Coalition for Community Centered Data Centers in Southern Maryland
summary: The Charles County Planning Commission voted to recommend denial of Zoning Text Amendment #25-187, which would have established zoning regulations for data centers in the county. Chairman Kevin Wedding cited concerns about higher electric bills for residents. Board member Denard Earl noted that Maryland is conducting a statewide data center impact study expected to conclude in September 2026, suggesting the county wait for those results. Board member Semia Hackett questioned whether approving one facility could open the door to additional projects. The recommendation now moves to the Board of Commissioners for final consideration.

Fulton County commissioners voted 2-1 to approve a one-year moratorium on data center construction, effective immediately. The vote followed a 6-1 recommendation from the Area Plan Commission on Feb. 10 after a standing-room-only public hearing. The moratorium targets a proposed 500MW data center by Decennial Group on 300+ acres near the town of Akron. Community members packed hearings expressing concerns about water and electricity strain. A data center committee will study economic and environmental impacts to guide future county regulations.

Pontiac City Council unanimously passed a six-month moratorium on the consideration of data centers 'pending further study and enactment of city regulation.' The resolution cited existing zoning insufficiency to prevent data centers' potential effects on public health, utilities, and infrastructure. City officials will review potential issues and propose a zoning ordinance during the moratorium period, which may be extended.

Hubbard Township trustees voted 2-1 to approve a resolution placing a one-year moratorium on data centers entering the township in February 2026. The township attorney reviewed the moratorium proposal and suggested it would be a wise idea, providing a 12-month window for the zoning commission to engage the public and develop appropriate regulations.

Vienna Township in northeast Ohio is using a pause on data centers to develop lasting zoning changes rather than a temporary moratorium. Trustee Phil Pegg requested zoning to place a limit of 85 decibels at the property line, and if a data center uses more than 5 megawatts of power, they must produce anything in excess on site or an adjacent site. All water usage and discharge must meet Ohio EPA regulations.

Oregon City Council initially voted 3-2 with one abstention against granting a six-month extension to a developer for a proposed $2 billion data center near Corduroy and Wynn roads. However, the city law director later ruled that under Robert's Rules of Order, the abstention didn't count as a vote, meaning the ordinance actually passed 3-2. The situation remains contentious and legally uncertain.

Air Products is appealing to the Upper Macungie Township Zoning Hearing Board for a proposed 2.6 million-square-foot, three-building data center complex on their former 194-acre headquarters site along Cetronia Road. The project predates the township's new data center ordinance adopted December 4, 2025, so it would be reviewed under old zoning rules. Air Products requested a delay until May 27, 2026 to decide whether to proceed. Township adopted data center regulations in December 2025 but they won't apply to this project.

Dickson City Borough Council adopted regulations that define, regulate and restrict data centers as special exceptions in the town's light manufacturing district, limiting data centers to Enterprise Street near Eagle Lane. The ordinance halted a proposed Bell Mountain data center campus. About 300 people attended the public hearing, with all 25 speakers either raising concerns about data centers or outright opposing them. Borough council stated 'We are not a sacrifice zone.'
company: Dickson City Development LLC / Kriger Construction

summary: The Tarboro Town Council rejected a Special Use Permit for Energy Storage Solutions' proposed 300MW, eight-phase data center on a 52-acre site near Anaconda and McNair roads by a 7-1 vote. The developer filed an appeal to Edgecombe Superior Court in November 2025, alleging the town committed 'errors of law.' A separate project proposed for the Kingsboro Business Park continues. Edgecombe County commissioners also voted down another ESS project. Residents cited concerns about traffic, water use, 24/7 noise, and proximity to the animal shelter.
opposition_groups: Edgecombe Neighbors for Data Center Accountability

summary: The New Hill Digital Campus proposed for an 89-acre site on Shearon Harris Road near the Harris Nuclear Plant faces massive community opposition. Nearly 5,000 people have signed a petition opposing the project. The facility would require ~250MW (25% of the nuclear plant's 1GW output) and up to 1 million gallons of reclaimed water per day. The town's wastewater facility is already nearing capacity. Apex Town Council spent 3+ hours examining the proposal; a decision is expected late summer/early fall 2026.

The largest permitted data center campus in the United States was approved in Pecos County in February 2026, developed by Pacifico Energy with approval from the Texas Commission on Environmental Quality. While the project has been approved, it represents a significant development in the Texas data center landscape. Local opposition details are limited compared to other TX fights, but the project is part of the broader Texas pushback environment.

Residents of Mount Horeb have rallied opposition to proposed data center construction by local officials. The community is among several Wisconsin towns (alongside Beaver Dam, Port Washington, Janesville, and others) pushing back against data center development. Part of the broader Wisconsin opposition movement where 70% of registered voters say data center costs outweigh benefits.

Senator Bernie Sanders called for a national moratorium on data center construction, aligning with Denver's moratorium announcement. Sanders argued that data centers are driving up electricity bills for American families while generating massive profits for tech companies. This was echoed by Ron DeSantis on the right, creating a bipartisan anti-data-center coalition. Over 230 state and local environmental groups sent a letter to Congress demanding a national moratorium in December 2025.

status: pending - in committee, gathering public testimony
summary: South Carolina S. 867, the 'Data Center Development Act', introduced January 29, 2026, is sponsored by Sens. Tom Davis (R-Beaufort, primary sponsor), Kimbrell, Garrett, Sutton, Walker, Blackmon, Turner, Zell, Kennedy, and Devine. The bill requires a state permit before a new data center can operate, and regulates permitted data centers on locations, water usage, infrastructure costs, and financial assurances. Sen. Davis described it as a 'middle ground' approach. Separately, H4583, the 'South Carolina Data Center Responsibility Act', is sponsored by Reps. April Cromer (R-Anderson), Gilreath, Oremus, Chumley, White, Reese, and Atkinson. Additional related bills include S. 724 (commercial data center water usage reporting) and S. 3401 (technology transparency). A gigawatt-scale data center proposal in the Lowcountry drew significant opposition from local residents and a bipartisan group of legislators representing Colleton County.

Armada Township enacted a 180-day moratorium on data center development starting January 14, 2026. The moratorium gives the township time to review zoning, infrastructure capacity, and land use impacts before any data center applications can proceed.
Clark Township enacted a 1-year moratorium on data center development in June 2025, making it one of the earliest communities in Michigan to take preemptive action against data center proposals.

Dundee Township enacted a 90-day moratorium on data center development in October 2025, later extended. The moratorium freezes all data center applications including those in progress. Cloverleaf Infrastructure retracted its pre-development agreement for a data center on a 750-acre site after public outcry. At a meeting in the Dundee Village office, dozens protested with the Village president stating that people do not want it.

Grand Blanc Township Board of Trustees passed a 1-year moratorium on data center development on January 21, 2026, joining the growing list of Michigan communities pausing data center approvals while developing appropriate zoning regulations.

Hayes Township (Charlevoix County) Board of Trustees unanimously enacted a 180-day moratorium on data centers on February 10, 2026, despite no current data center proposals. After a full hour of public comment in person and over Zoom, the board struck exemption clauses that the public identified as possible loopholes. The board is drafting data center regulations for future zoning codes.

Lenox Township Board of Trustees adopted a 4-month moratorium on new data center development effective February 2, 2026, with the option for one additional 4-month extension. The moratorium gives the township time to review zoning, infrastructure capacity, utility demands, and land use impacts. The township emphasized that under Michigan law, municipalities cannot use zoning or moratoria to permanently block lawful development (exclusionary zoning).

Lodi Township (Washtenaw County) enacted a 180-day moratorium on data center development on February 3, 2026. Located near other Washtenaw County communities opposing data centers including Saline, Pittsfield Township, and York Township.

Manchester Township enacted a 2-year moratorium on data center development on October 14, 2025 -- one of the longest moratorium durations among Michigan communities. The township is located in Washtenaw County near several other communities that have also enacted moratoria.
opposition_groups: Economic Development Responsibility Alliance of Michigan

summary: Northville City Council unanimously approved a 12-month moratorium on data centers and similar industrial facilities on January 6, 2026, making it the first Southeast Michigan community to take proactive action before any proposals emerged. The pause allows proper planning for construction standards, environmental protections, and emergency preparedness.

Pittsfield Township trustees voted on a 180-day moratorium on any data center proposals on November 18, 2025. Supervisor Trish Reilly noted that the township's zoning ordinance does not address data centers. There are currently no known plans to build a data center in the township, but officials enacted the moratorium preemptively.

Saginaw City Council unanimously approved a resolution enacting a 6-month blanket prohibition of data centers on January 13, 2026. Council members aim to finalize a comprehensive data center policy within that timeframe to ensure responsible development that benefits the community long term.

South Lyon City Council adopted a 12-month moratorium on new data center applications on February 10, 2026, extending it beyond the original proposal of six months to match a similar pause recently enacted in Northville.

Springfield Township (Oakland County) Board of Trustees approved a 180-day moratorium on data center plans on January 7, 2026, three days after planners with consulting firm Giffels Webster told the planning commission the township was 'unprepared for the growing economic pressure to construct new data centers.' A 'long-established data center development group' is interested in an 84-acre tract in the Andersonville Road area. During the moratorium, officials will determine where data centers can be located, establish construction and cybersecurity standards, protect residential areas, and require environmental and utility studies.

Sylvan Township (near Chelsea, Washtenaw County) enacted a 6-month moratorium on data centers on January 22, 2026. Supervisor Amanda Nimke stated: 'We clearly can't stop them, but we can control them in a way that they're not going to be proposed in an area that will undermine a community.'

Taylor enacted a 1-year data center moratorium in January 2026. State Sen. Erika Geiss (D-Taylor) also introduced Senate Bills 761, 762, and 763 in December to bar data centers from withdrawing more than 2 million gallons of water a day for consumptive use and to create transparency standards for water and energy use.

Tyrone Township (Livingston County) enacted a 6-month moratorium on data center development on December 2, 2025. The township is located near Howell Township, where a major $1 billion Meta data center proposal generated significant community opposition.

The Village of Romeo enacted a data center moratorium on January 26, 2026, joining the wave of Macomb County communities (including nearby Armada Township, Lenox Township, and Sterling Heights) in pausing data center development while drafting appropriate regulations.

York Township (Washtenaw County) enacted a 6-month moratorium on data center development on February 12, 2026, after representatives for developer Sansone Group discussed a possible 1-gigawatt data center on 200 acres of property owned by Toyota at the corner of Willis and Platt roads. The proposed site is roughly 6 miles from Augusta Township's potential $1B data center, 10 miles from the Oracle/OpenAI $7B Saline Township project, and 8 miles from the UMich/Los Alamos Ypsilanti Township data center.

Solon Township (Kent County) Board unanimously approved a 6-month moratorium on new data center proposals in early February 2026, extended from an initial 90-day proposal after hearing residents' concerns. Developers are exploring farmland west of US-131, between 16 and 17 Mile roads, as a potential data center site. The property is currently zoned agricultural/residential.

Dorr Township (Allegan County) enacted a moratorium on data center development while the planning commission develops an ordinance to regulate the facilities, expected to take about six months. Microsoft purchased nearly 272 acres of land off 144th Avenue near US 131 in 2024 for more than $48 million, with 128 acres planned for data center development. Microsoft terminated its NDA with Dorr Township in late February 2026. More than 50 residents attended a December 18 board meeting raising concerns about traffic, electricity, and water impacts.
opposition_groups: Dorr Township MI - We The People

summary: Lyon Township (Oakland County) board voted unanimously to enact a 180-day moratorium on data centers and all proposed developments 100,000+ sq ft on March 3, 2026. The moratorium followed months of community opposition to 'Project Flex,' a 1.8 million sq ft AI data center by California-based developer Verrus approved by the planning commission in September 2025 on 172 acres zoned industrial. Residents didn't learn about the project until December. The moratorium does not affect Project Flex, only newly proposed data centers.
opposition_groups: NO DATA CENTER in Lyon Township

summary: Caledonia Township (Kent County) implemented a 6-month moratorium on data center and data mining operations effective January 1, 2026, running through June.
