The moratorium follows inquiries from data center developers about property at 68th Street and Kraft Avenue. The township is considering requiring special use permits for data centers to give the community more control over their applications and approval.

Erie Township (Monroe County) enacted a limited moratorium on data center development on February 10, 2026, joining nearby Dundee Township (also in Monroe County) in restricting data center proposals while developing zoning regulations.

Ypsilanti City Council held a public hearing on March 3 to consider both a 60-day emergency moratorium and a separate 365-day resolution on data center zoning permits. The 60-day measure comes from city administration as a preventive step, while the 365-day resolution was introduced by Ward 3 Council member Amber Fellows. This is a separate action from the earlier Ypsilanti Township petition.
company: University of Michigan / Los Alamos National Laboratory

summary: Boone Town Council discussed a moratorium on data center construction, with Mayor Dalton George saying the town should be proactive and 'recognize the environmental damage that these data centers and similar uses like crypto-mining can do.' A public hearing is scheduled for March 23, 2026, to further discuss the moratorium. Modeled after Canton's 12-month moratorium.

Columbus City Council discussed a proposed $5.18 billion, 900-acre data center campus (Project Ruby) in northeast Muscogee County near Harris and Talbot County lines. About half the citizens at the meeting voiced concerns about environmental and community impacts. A Change.org petition opposes the project. Developer Habitat Real Estate Partners selected Layfield Road for the facility, which would generate 195 permanent jobs and up to $68.7M/year in property taxes by 2030. Next public meeting is March 6, 2026.

Weld County commissioners are developing a data center zoning ordinance (Code Ordinance 2026-01) that would limit data centers to industrial or agricultural zones and require site plan or special review. Triggered by Global AI's purchase of 438 acres near Windsor for a data center. The county extended the timeline for the ordinance's final reading to allow more public input. Facilities under 50MW total capacity would fall under these rules.

Township supervisors voted 3-2 to reject a 120,000 sq ft, three-story 'boutique' data center on 18 acres off Route 724 before the proposal was even formally submitted. Over 100 residents attended, mostly in opposition. Developer Envision Land Use subsequently abandoned the plan entirely. The proposed site was at 299 W. Schuylkill Road, adjacent to Route 100 near a PECO substation.

Planning commission meeting was overwhelmed when too many residents showed up to oppose an expanded data center proposal, forcing officials to reschedule to March 9 at a larger venue. Sentinel Data Centers and Green Fig Land LLC seek to expand a previously approved data center by 61% to over 1.6 million sq ft on roughly 100 acres at 13 S. Bacton Hill Rd. The project straddles East and West Whiteland townships.
company: Sentinel Data Centers / Green Fig Land LLC

summary: Residents are challenging the validity of Tobyhanna Township's data center overlay zoning before the Zoning Hearing Board in Monroe County. The hearing is scheduled for March 11, 2026 at 5pm. Part of a broader grassroots resistance movement in Pennsylvania against over 50 proposed data center projects across the state.
opposition_groups: Tobyhanna Township Residents Against Caughbaugh Road Data Center

summary: Despite growing opposition, Middlesex Township Planning Commission approved Phase 1-A of a proposed $15 billion, 700-acre, 18-building data center complex (Pennsylvania Digital 1/PAX-1) on Country Club Road near Carlisle. Led by Carlisle Development Partners LLC. The plan goes to the Board of Supervisors in March. Would create ~450 jobs and generate $65M in tax revenue. Community concerns center on massive water and power demands in a rural area.
company: Carlisle Development Partners / Powerhouse Data Centers

summary: Urbana City Council voted 6-1 to approve a 12-month moratorium on data centers over 10,000 sq ft, halting a proposed 460,000 sq ft Thor Equities facility off Routes 55 and 68. Over 40 citizens spoke at the meeting. The site consists of three parcels purchased for $5 million. Concerns center on impacts to Cedar Bog, water, electricity, noise, and property values. The only dissenting vote came from the mayor's wife.

Royalton voters approved a five-year moratorium on AI and crypto data centers via show-of-hands vote at Town Meeting Day. About 154 of the town's registered voters participated. No data centers have been proposed for the town; the ban is preemptive. The Planning Commission will write the policy into the town plan for Selectboard approval. State Senator Becca White spoke at the meeting about her statewide moratorium bill (S. 205).

Elmwood Township Board unanimously approved a 9-month moratorium on data centers (reduced from originally proposed 12 months). No known data center plans exist for the county; the moratorium is precautionary. The board tabled the measure at its January 21 meeting before passing the amended version in February.

Ann Arbor Township Board of Trustees passed a 12-month moratorium on data centers. No developer has approached township officials about a data center; the moratorium is a proactive measure reflecting concerns about water, energy demand, and environmental impacts seen in neighboring communities.

Anchorage Assembly held public hearing on Ordinance AO 2026-27 to amend Title 21 of the Municipal Code, defining data centers as a distinct land use and restricting them to conditional approval in industrial, port, airport, and public lands zones. Requires landscaping buffers, setbacks, enclosed power equipment, and detailed impact reviews. Current definitions date to the 1990s. Next public hearing scheduled March 24.

Mooringsport Mayor Tyler Gordon, Michael Craft, and Mary Blakemore filed a petition for judicial review in Caddo District Court challenging the Shreveport City Council's approval of a special use permit for a TXLAAR LLC data center. The lawsuit alleges the city violated transparency laws by using NDAs, and that the facility would use up to 7.5 million gallons of water/day and discharge 2.5 million gallons of contaminated wastewater daily. The Sierra Club Delta Chapter supports the lawsuit and is working to join as co-plaintiffs. The facility would include seven 400,000 sq ft buildings.

Rural communities in North Caddo Parish organized a boycott of the official data center community meeting in Blanchard on March 3. Residents near Caddo Lake oppose a proposed data center campus on State Line Road and Blanchard Latex Road near Mooringsport. State Rep. Danny McCormick has demanded transparency around NDAs signed by local officials. The Village of Mooringsport unanimously approved a resolution on Feb. 12 calling for an environmental study. Caddo Commission declined to vote on a one-year water moratorium proposed by Commissioner Chris Kracman.

Food & Water Watch released 'The Urgent Case Against Data Centers,' a comprehensive first-of-its-kind report making the case for a nationwide moratorium on new AI-driven data center construction. Released the same day President Trump hosted a White House signing ceremony with seven Big Tech companies pledging to reduce AI energy impacts. Report highlights water/power consumption, fossil fuel demand, air/water pollution risks, loss of farmland, and noise pollution.

Delta County Planning Commission considered a resolution to suspend data center and cryptocurrency mining projects for 12 months while a zoning ordinance is developed. Commission Chair Amy Berglund said no known data center plans exist for the county but cited concerns over utility demand and water usage. Final decision expected in April 2026.
